Urukrama (as son of Nābhi and Merudevī)

Urukrama (as son of Nābhi and Merudevī)

edges.rel: has body parts from which deities emerge

beats.title: The Sages' Inquiry
beats.summary: The chapter begins with the sages in Naimiṣāraṇya asking Sūta to narrate the essence of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa, focusing on the avatāras of Lord Kṛṣṇa, especially his pastimes with Rāma. They express the need for spiritual knowledge in the Kali Yuga and request Sūta to describe how dharma survives after Kṛṣṇa's departure.

beats.title: The Questions of the Sages
beats.summary: The first chapter of the Bhagavata Purana begins with the sages at Naimiṣāraṇya asking Sūta to narrate the essence of all scriptures, focusing on the divine pastimes of Lord Kṛṣṇa, who incarnates for the welfare of the world. They inquire about the supreme dharma and the means to attain liberation, emphasizing the power of hearing the Lord's glories.

beats.title: The Supreme Dharma: Bhakti Yoga
beats.summary: Suta Gosvami begins answering the sages' questions, emphasizing that the supreme dharma is bhakti (devotion) to Lord Vasudeva (Krishna), which leads to detachment, knowledge, and liberation. He explains the three modes of nature (sattva, rajas, tamas) and how the Lord manifests as Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva for creation, maintenance, and destruction. The chapter concludes by describing how the Lord, though unaffected by the modes, appears to act through them.

beats.title: The Questions of the Sages and the Glories of Bhakti
beats.summary: Suta Gosvami, in response to the sages' questions, explains the supreme dharma of devotion to Vasudeva (Krishna), the process of bhakti-yoga, and the nature of the Supreme Truth as Brahman, Paramatma, and Bhagavan. He emphasizes that bhakti purifies the heart, destroys karma, and leads to liberation. The chapter also describes the three modes of material nature and their corresponding deities, and concludes that all Vedic activities culminate in Vasudeva.

beats.title: The Avatars of the Lord
beats.summary: This chapter enumerates the primary avatars of Lord Vishnu, from the first Purusha incarnation to Kalki, and concludes with the glory of the Bhagavata Purana. It emphasizes that all beings are parts of the Lord, and that Krishna is the Supreme Personality of Godhead.

beats.title: The Avatars of the Lord
beats.summary: This chapter enumerates the primary avatars of Lord Vishnu, from the first Purusha incarnation to Kalki, and concludes with the glory of the Bhagavata Purana. It describes how the Lord manifests in various forms for creation, preservation, and destruction, and emphasizes that all beings are parts of His potency.

beats.title: The Compilation of the Vedas and the Dissatisfaction of Vyasa
beats.summary: This chapter describes the compilation of the Vedas and the Puranas by Vyasa, his dissatisfaction despite his works, and the arrival of Narada to guide him. It also includes the context of the Bhagavata Purana being spoken by Shuka to Parikshit, as requested by Shaunaka and other sages.

beats.title: The Quest for the Bhāgavata
beats.summary: Śaunaka asks Sūta about the Bhāgavata Purāṇa, the life of Śuka, and the circumstances of Vyāsa's composition. Sūta recounts how Vyāsa, seeing the degraded state of humanity in the Kali Yuga, compiled the Vedas and wrote the Mahābhārata, but still felt incomplete. Nārada then visits him.

beats.title: Nārada's Instructions to Vyāsa
beats.summary: Narada instructs Vyasa on the necessity of glorifying the Supreme Lord, especially through the Bhāgavata Purāṇa, to attain true satisfaction and liberation. He emphasizes that devotional service to Krishna surpasses all other practices, and narrates his own past life as a servant of sages, which led to his enlightenment.

beats.title: Narada's Instructions to Vyasa
beats.summary: Narada instructs Vyasa on the necessity of glorifying the Supreme Lord's pastimes, emphasizing that mere dharma, artha, kama, and moksha without bhakti are incomplete. He narrates his own past life as a servant's son who attained devotion through association with sages and hearing Krishna-katha, and advises Vyasa to compose the Bhagavata Purana to describe the Lord's transcendental activities.

beats.title: Narada's Past Life and Attainment of Siddhi
beats.summary: Narada narrates his past life: as a young boy, his mother died, and he wandered alone, eventually meditating on Vishnu in a forest. He had a vision of the Lord, who taught him about devotion and the path of bhakti. Narada then traveled the world singing the Lord's glories, and at death, he attained a divine body and became a celestial sage, roaming freely by the grace of Mahavishnu.

beats.title: The Aftermath of War: Aśvatthāmā's Capture and Draupadī's Mercy
beats.summary: Sūta narrates how Vyāsa, after Nārada's departure, composed the Bhāgavata Purāṇa through bhakti-yoga and taught it to his son Śuka. The chapter then recounts the aftermath of the Kurukṣetra war: Aśvatthāmā kills the sleeping sons of Draupadī; Arjuna pursues and captures him; Draupadī, despite her grief, pleads for his release; Kṛṣṇa instructs Arjuna to fulfill his vow without killing Aśvatthāmā; Arjuna shaves his head and takes his jewel, banishing him; the Pāṇḍavas perform funeral rites.

beats.title: Kuntī's Prayer and Krishna's Departure
beats.summary: After the war, Kunti and the women go to the Ganges to offer water to the dead. Krishna consoles them, then prepares to return to Dvārakā. Uttara, pregnant, seeks protection from Aśvatthāmā's brahmāstra. Krishna protects her womb. Kunti prays to Krishna, recounting his protection and expressing her desire for constant remembrance. Dhṛtarāṣṭra remains grief-stricken despite teachings. Yudhiṣṭhira laments his sin of killing relatives.

beats.title: Bhīṣma's Death and Instructions
beats.summary: Bhīṣma lies on a bed of arrows, surrounded by sages, Pāṇḍavas, and Kṛṣṇa. He teaches Yudhiṣṭhira about dharma, then fixes his mind on Kṛṣṇa and dies, attaining liberation. The chapter highlights Bhīṣma's devotion and the supremacy of Kṛṣṇa.

beats.title: Kṛṣṇa's Departure from Hastinapura
beats.summary: After the war, Yudhiṣṭhira rules righteously, and Kṛṣṇa stays in Hastinapura to comfort the family. When Kṛṣṇa departs, the Pāṇḍavas and others are grief-stricken. The women of the city praise Kṛṣṇa's divine nature and his pastimes. Kṛṣṇa travels back to Dvārakā, passing through various regions.

beats.title: Krishna's Return to Dvārakā
beats.summary: Lord Krishna returns to Dvārakā after the Kurukshetra war. The citizens joyfully welcome him, and he enters the city, greeted by Vasudeva, Akrūra, Ugrasena, Balarāma, and others. He pays respects to his mothers, especially Devakī, and then meets his wives. The chapter describes the opulence of Dvārakā and the devotion of its people, concluding with reflections on Krishna's divine nature and his pastimes.

beats.title: Chapter 12
beats.summary: This chapter describes the birth of Parīkṣit, the son of Abhimanyu and Uttarā, who was saved in the womb by Lord Kṛṣṇa from the brahmāstra of Aśvatthāmā. It narrates his naming by the brāhmaṇas as Viṣṇurāta, his upbringing, and Yudhiṣṭhira's performance of three Aśvamedha sacrifices with Kṛṣṇa's presence. The chapter also foretells Parīkṣit's future as a righteous king and his eventual renunciation.

beats.title: Vidura's Counsel and Dhritarashtra's Renunciation
beats.summary: Vidura returns to Hastinapura after pilgrimage and is warmly welcomed. He advises Dhritarashtra to renounce worldly attachments and leave for the forest, as death is imminent. Dhritarashtra, with Gandhari, departs for the Himalayas. Yudhishthira, distressed by their departure, is consoled by Narada, who explains the nature of time and the body, and reveals that Dhritarashtra will attain liberation through yoga and self-immolation.

beats.title: Yudhiṣṭhira's Anxiety and Arjuna's Return
beats.summary: Yudhiṣṭhira observes ominous omens and becomes anxious about Arjuna's prolonged absence from Dvārakā. He questions Arjuna upon his return about the welfare of the Vṛṣṇi clan and Kṛṣṇa, sensing that something is wrong.

beats.title: The Pandavas' Renunciation and Liberation
beats.summary: Arjuna laments the departure of Krishna, recounting his past glories and the protection Krishna provided. He realizes that without Krishna, all his power and possessions are meaningless. The chapter describes the aftermath of Krishna's departure: the onset of Kali Yuga, Yudhishthira's renunciation, the Pandavas' journey northward, and their attainment of liberation through devotion.

beats.title: Parīkṣit's Reign and the Lament of Earth and Dharma
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit, after being saved from Aśvatthāmā's brahmāstra, rules the earth righteously. He performs three Aśvamedha sacrifices and subdues Kali in disguise. The chapter then describes a conversation between Dharma (in bull form) and Earth (as a cow), where Earth laments the loss of virtue due to Kali's influence and the departure of Lord Kṛṣṇa.

beats.title: Parīkṣit Encounters Kali and Restores Dharma
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit encounters a bull (Dharma) and a cow (Earth) being beaten by a Śūdra disguised as a king (Kali). He confronts the aggressor, learns that Kali has broken three of Dharma's four legs (tapas, śauca, dayā), leaving only satya. Parīkṣit spares Kali's life but banishes him to five places: gambling, drinking, prostitution, animal slaughter, and gold. He then restores Dharma and Earth.

beats.title: The Curse of Parikshit
beats.summary: Suta narrates the story of King Parikshit, who, while hunting, insults a meditating sage by placing a dead snake on his shoulder. The sage's son curses Parikshit to die by snakebite in seven days. The sage, however, rebukes his son and praises Parikshit as a great devotee of Krishna.

beats.title: The Penance of Parīkṣit
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit, cursed to die by a snake bite, renounces his kingdom and sits on the bank of the Ganges to fast unto death. Many sages gather around him, and he asks them about the duties of a dying person. The sage Śuka, son of Vyāsa, arrives and is honored by Parīkṣit.

beats.title: The Cosmic Form as Support for Meditation
beats.summary: Shuka begins answering Parikshit's question about the duty of a man facing imminent death. He emphasizes that the highest purpose of human life is to remember Narayana at the end, and describes the process of meditation on the cosmic form of the Lord as a means to achieve that. The chapter includes a detailed description of the virat-rupa (universal form) as a support for meditation.

beats.title: The Path of Yoga and Bhakti
beats.summary: This chapter describes the process of meditation and yoga leading to liberation, emphasizing the importance of focusing on the form of the Lord (Vishnu) and the path of bhakti-yoga. It outlines the stages of yogic practice, including control of the mind and prana, and the ultimate attainment of the supreme abode of Vishnu.

beats.title: Desires and Devotion
beats.summary: This chapter of the Bhagavata Purana, narrated by Shuka to Parikshit, lists various material desires and the corresponding deities to worship for their fulfillment, culminating in the teaching that one who is desireless or seeks liberation should worship the Supreme Lord through intense bhakti-yoga. It then emphasizes the importance of hearing and chanting the glories of the Lord, criticizing those who do not engage in such practices as living like animals.

beats.title: Parīkṣit's Inquiry and Śukadeva's Praise of the Lord
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit, having heard the teachings of Śukadeva, renounces attachment to family, wealth, and kingdom, and develops firm devotion to Vāsudeva. He then asks Śukadeva to explain how the Lord creates, sustains, and dissolves the universe through His māyā. Śukadeva responds with a series of prayers to the Supreme Lord, describing His glories and the path of surrender.

beats.title: Brahma's Discourse on Creation
beats.summary: Brahma explains to Narada the process of creation, starting from the Supreme Lord Vasudeva/Narayana, through the three gunas, the mahat-tattva, ahamkara, the five elements, the senses, and the cosmic egg, culminating in the manifestation of the universal form of Purusha and the planetary systems.

beats.title: The Cosmic Body of the Purusha and the First Sacrifice
beats.summary: Brahma describes the cosmic body of the Supreme Purusha, mapping various cosmic elements and deities to parts of the body. He explains how the entire universe, including all beings and sacrificial elements, are manifestations of the Purusha. He recounts his own creation from the lotus navel of the Purusha and how he performed the first sacrifice using the body's parts. He concludes by praising the Supreme Lord, whose incarnations are enumerated.

beats.title: The Avatars of Vishnu
beats.summary: Brahma describes the various avatars of Vishnu, including the boar, Kapila, Dattatreya, the four Kumaras, Nara-Narayana, Dhruva, Prithu, Rishabha, Hayashira, Matsya, Kurma, Narasimha, Vamana, Parashurama, Rama, Krishna, and Kalki, along with their deeds and teachings. The chapter emphasizes the supremacy of Vishnu and the path of devotion.

beats.title: Parīkṣit's Inquiry and Śuka's Response
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about the nature of the universe, time, creation, avatars, dharma, and liberation, expressing his desire to hear the glories of Kṛṣṇa. Śuka begins to narrate the Bhāgavata Purāṇa as taught by the Lord to Brahmā.

beats.title: The Vision of Vaikuṇṭha and the Teaching on Māyā
beats.summary: Lord Vishnu reveals the nature of His māyā to Brahmā, who then teaches this knowledge to Nārada, who in turn imparts it to Vyāsa. The chapter emphasizes that the Supreme Lord alone exists before creation, and everything else is His māyā. It also describes Brahmā's penance, his vision of Vaikuṇṭha, and the Lord's instruction on the means to create without entanglement.

beats.title: The Ten Topics of the Bhāgavata and the Cosmic Creation
beats.summary: This chapter defines the ten topics of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa: sarga (primary creation), visarga (secondary creation), sthāna (maintenance), poṣaṇa (protection), ūtaya (desires), manvantara (Manu periods), īśānukathā (stories of the Lord), nirodha (dissolution), mukti (liberation), and āśraya (ultimate refuge). It then describes the cosmic creation from the Supreme Person, the evolution of the senses and elements, and the classification of beings according to the three guṇas. The chapter concludes with a transition to the story of Vidura's pilgrimage.

beats.title: Vidura's Pilgrimage and Meeting with Uddhava
beats.summary: This chapter recounts Vidura's pilgrimage after being insulted by Duryodhana, his meeting with Uddhava, and his inquiries about the well-being of Krishna and the Yadus. It also includes the story of Dhritarashtra's blindness to his sons' adharma and the events leading to the Kurukshetra war.

beats.title: Uddhava's Lament and Krishna's Pastimes
beats.summary: Uddhava, overwhelmed by grief after Krishna's departure, recounts to Vidura the pastimes of the Lord in Vrindavan and Dvaraka, highlighting His mercy even to enemies and the depth of devotion.

beats.title: Krishna's Pastimes and the End of the Yadava Dynasty
beats.summary: Uddhava narrates Krishna's pastimes: his return to Mathura, killing of Kamsa, education from Sandipani, marriage to Rukmini and others, killing of demons, the Kurukshetra war, establishment of Yudhishthira, and the eventual destruction of the Yadava dynasty due to a curse, leading to Krishna's departure.

beats.title: Uddhava's Departure and Vidura's Inquiry
beats.summary: Uddhava recounts to Vidura the events after the Yadu clan's destruction: Krishna's departure from the mortal world, his instructions to Uddhava to go to Badarika ashrama, and Uddhava's meeting with the sage Maitreya. Vidura, hearing of Krishna's pastimes and departure, becomes overwhelmed with devotion.

beats.title: Vidura's Inquiry and Maitreya's Discourse on Creation
beats.summary: Vidura approaches the sage Maitreya at the bank of the Ganges and asks about the nature of the Supreme Lord, the process of creation, and the means to attain liberation. Maitreya praises Vidura's devotion and begins to explain the cosmic creation, starting from the unmanifest to the mahat-tattva, ahaṅkāra, and the five gross elements. The demigods, feeling helpless, pray to the Lord for guidance.

beats.title: Cosmic Creation from the Virāṭ Puruṣa
beats.summary: The ṛṣi describes how the Supreme Lord, as Kāla (Time), enters the aggregate of twenty-three elements, awakening them to create the cosmic being (Hiraṇmaya Puruṣa). From the cosmic being's body parts, various deities and worlds emerge, along with the four varṇas from different limbs. The chapter concludes by extolling the Lord's māyā and offering obeisances.

beats.title: Vidura's Inquiry and Maitreya's Discourse on Creation and Liberation
beats.summary: Vidura asks Maitreya about the nature of the Supreme Lord's activities, the creation and dissolution of the universe, and the paths to liberation. Maitreya explains that the Lord's māyā is the cause of apparent contradictions, and that through bhakti-yoga one can overcome ignorance. He then describes the cosmic creation, the avatars, and various topics including dharma, yoga, and the purpose of scriptures.

beats.title: The Vision of Brahma and the Lineage of the Bhagavata
beats.summary: Maitreya recounts the lineage of the Bhagavata Purana and describes the cosmic vision of Brahma, who, at the dawn of creation, sees the Supreme Lord Vishnu reclining on Ananta Shesha in the causal waters, and then performs a meditation to understand his own origin.

beats.title: Brahma's Hymn and Vishnu's Instruction
beats.summary: Brahma, having emerged from the lotus arising from Vishnu's navel, offers a hymn of praise to Vishnu, expressing his realization of the Lord's supremacy and his own dependence. Vishnu responds by instructing Brahma to perform penance and acquire knowledge, promising that through devotion he will perceive the Lord's presence in all beings. Vishnu then disappears, and Brahma begins his creative work.

beats.title: The Nine Creations and the Cosmic Order
beats.summary: Maitreya describes the process of creation by Brahma after the Lord's departure, including the nine types of creation (sarga) and the enumeration of various beings and species.

beats.title: The Measurement of Time
beats.summary: Maitreya explains the measurement of time from the atomic level to the cosmic scale, including the duration of yugas, manvantaras, and kalpas, culminating in the description of the two parārdhas of Brahmā's life and the cosmic egg.

beats.title: The Creation of Rudra and the Prajapatis
beats.summary: Maitreya describes the creation of the universe by Brahma, including the birth of Rudra from Brahma's anger, the naming of Rudra and his various forms, the creation of the ten Prajapatis, the emergence of the Vedas and other scriptures, the origin of the Manu Svayambhuva and his wife Shatarupa, and the beginning of procreation.

beats.title: The Boar Incarnation: Lifting the Earth
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of the boar incarnation of Lord Vishnu (Varaha) who lifts the Earth from the cosmic waters. It begins with Vidura asking Maitreya about the deeds of Svayambhuva Manu. Maitreya recounts how Manu, along with his wife, prayed to Brahma for guidance. Brahma instructed Manu to rule the Earth and perform sacrifices. When the Earth sank into the waters, Brahma meditated, and from his nostril emerged a tiny boar that grew to gigantic size. The boar, identified as Lord Vishnu, plunged into the waters, killed the demon Hiranyaksha, and lifted the Earth on its tusks. The sages and Brahma praised the Lord in hymns describing his Vedic form.

beats.title: The Curse and Blessing of Diti's Sons
beats.summary: Vidura asks Maitreya about the story of Hiranyaksha's death and the boar incarnation. Maitreya narrates how Diti, desiring children, approached her husband Kashyapa at an inauspicious time. Despite his warnings, she insisted, and they conceived. Kashyapa prophesied that her sons would be demons who would torment the world, but one grandson would be a great devotee. Diti prayed for her sons to be killed by the Lord, not by a brahmana's curse.

beats.title: The Curse of Jaya and Vijaya
beats.summary: The chapter describes the creation of the demon Hiranyakashipu from Diti's womb, the gods' plea to Brahma, Brahma's narration of the story of the four Kumaras' visit to Vaikuntha, their confrontation with the gatekeepers Jaya and Vijaya, the curse by the Kumaras, and the subsequent appearance of Lord Vishnu to pacify the situation.

beats.title: The Curse of Jaya and Vijaya
beats.summary: The Lord explains to the sages that his gatekeepers Jaya and Vijaya, who cursed them, were acting under his will. He accepts the sages' curse as his own and decrees that Jaya and Vijaya will take three births as demons before returning. The sages praise the Lord's protection of brahmanas and accept the curse. The Lord then sends Jaya and Vijaya to take birth as Hiranyakashipu and Hiranyaksha.

beats.title: The Birth of Hiranyakashipu and Hiranyaksha
beats.summary: Maitreya narrates the birth of the twin Daityas Hiranyakashipu and Hiranyaksha from Diti, accompanied by terrifying omens. The demons grow immensely powerful, and Hiranyaksha challenges Varuna, who directs him to Vishnu as the only worthy opponent.

beats.title: The Battle of Varaha and Hiranyaksha
beats.summary: The chapter describes the battle between Lord Vishnu in the form of Varaha (the boar) and the demon Hiranyaksha. After lifting the earth from the cosmic waters, Varaha is challenged by Hiranyaksha, who mocks and attacks him. A fierce duel ensues, witnessed by Brahma and other sages. Brahma urges Varaha to slay the demon quickly before the inauspicious twilight hour passes.

beats.title: The Slaying of Hiraṇyākṣa
beats.summary: The chapter describes the battle between Lord Vishnu as the boar incarnation (Yajña-sūkara) and the demon Hiraṇyākṣa. After a fierce fight, Hiraṇyākṣa is slain, and the gods praise the Lord. The chapter concludes with the narrator Vidura's joy upon hearing the story and the benefits of reciting it.

beats.title: Brahma's Creation of Beings
beats.summary: Vidura asks Maitreya about the creation of beings by Brahma. Maitreya describes how Brahma, after creating the cosmic egg, generates various beings from different parts of his body, including demons, gods, gandharvas, apsaras, and others, each arising from his mental states and actions.

beats.title: The Meeting of Kardama and Manu
beats.summary: Vidura asks Maitreya about the progeny of Svayambhuva Manu. Maitreya narrates how Kardama, instructed by Brahma to create progeny, performed penance at Sarasvati's bank and was blessed by Lord Vishnu. Vishnu promised that Manu would offer his daughter Devahuti to Kardama, and that Vishnu would incarnate as Kapila to teach Sankhya. Kardama waited, and Manu arrived with Devahuti. The chapter describes the hermitage of Kardama and the meeting.

beats.title: The Marriage of Kardama and Devahuti
beats.summary: King Manu approaches the sage Kardama to offer his daughter Devahuti in marriage. Kardama accepts, and the wedding is performed with great ceremony. Manu returns to his capital Barhishmati, where he rules righteously, devoted to Vishnu, and passes his time in hearing and chanting the Lord's glories.

beats.title: Kardama and Devahūti: Divine Union and Renunciation
beats.summary: Kardama Muni, pleased by his wife Devahūti's devoted service, grants her divine blessings and creates a celestial palace. After bathing in a holy lake, Devahūti is transformed and reunited with Kardama. They travel in a flying palace, enjoying celestial pleasures for a hundred years. Devahūti bears nine daughters. When Kardama prepares to renounce, Devahūti expresses her fear of separation and seeks his protection, leading to teachings on detachment and the value of association with saints.

beats.title: The Birth of Kapila and Kardama's Renunciation
beats.summary: Kardama Prajapati, having fulfilled his duty of procreation, seeks liberation. His wife Devahuti gives birth to Kapila, an incarnation of Vishnu. Brahma visits, instructs Kardama to give his daughters in marriage to the sages, and reveals Kapila's divine nature. Kardama then renounces, practices yoga, and attains liberation.

beats.title: Kapila Instructs Devahūti on Bhakti-Yoga
beats.summary: Kapila instructs his mother Devahūti on the path of bhakti-yoga, emphasizing that pure devotion to the Supreme Lord is the highest means to liberation, surpassing jñāna and vairāgya. He describes the nature of the self, the bondage caused by attachment to the guṇas, and the freedom attained through fixing the mind on the Lord.

beats.title: Cosmic Evolution: From Prakṛti to Virāṭ
beats.summary: Lord Kapila explains the 24 principles of material creation to Devahūti, detailing the evolution from prakṛti and puruṣa through mahat-tattva, ahaṅkāra, mind, senses, and gross elements, culminating in the formation of the cosmic egg and the emergence of Virāṭ Puruṣa.

beats.title: The Path to Liberation Through Bhakti and Knowledge
beats.summary: Lord Kapila explains to Devahūti the path to liberation through bhakti-yoga, jñāna, and vairāgya, emphasizing that the puruṣa (self) is unaffected by prakṛti's guṇas when established in knowledge. He describes the process of detachment, the role of the mind, and the ultimate attainment of kaivalya (liberation) through devotion to the Supreme.

beats.title: The Yoga of Meditation on the Lord
beats.summary: Lord Kapila describes the yoga of meditation (sabīja yoga) to Devahūti, detailing the path of purification through yama, niyama, āsana, prāṇāyāma, pratyāhāra, dhāraṇā, and dhyāna on the form of the Lord. The culmination is samādhi, where the yogī realizes the self as distinct from the body and mind, and attains liberation.

beats.title: The Path of Bhakti-Yoga
beats.summary: In this chapter, Devahūti asks Kapila (the Lord) about the nature of bhakti-yoga and the means to attain detachment. Kapila responds by describing the three types of bhakti (tamasic, rajasic, sattvic) and the supreme nirguṇa bhakti. He emphasizes that true devotion recognizes the Lord in all beings, and that worship of the deity without respect for living beings is futile. He also explains the hierarchy of beings and the nature of time as a manifestation of the Lord.

beats.title: The Torments of the Sinful Soul
beats.summary: Kapila describes the futile struggles of the embodied soul entangled in family life, the inevitable suffering of old age and death, the terrifying journey to Yamaloka, and the various hellish torments awaiting the sinful. He emphasizes that the soul alone reaps the consequences of its actions, and that hellish experiences are also reflected in earthly suffering.

beats.title: The Suffering of the Fetus in the Womb
beats.summary: This chapter describes the development of the fetus in the womb month by month, the intense suffering experienced by the embodied soul during gestation, and the prayer of the fetus (jantu) to the Supreme Lord, seeking liberation from the cycle of birth and death. The chapter ends with Kapila's introduction.

beats.title: Kapila's Discourse on the Paths of Yoga and Bhakti
beats.summary: Kapila describes the suffering of the soul in the womb and childhood, the dangers of attachment to women and sense objects, the paths of karma-yoga and jnana-yoga, and the supreme goal of bhakti-yoga leading to liberation.

beats.title: Devahūti's Liberation and Kapila's Departure
beats.summary: Devahūti praises Kapila, who then instructs her on the path to liberation. She follows his teachings, attains self-realization, and merges with Brahman. Her body transforms into the river Siddhidā, and Kapila departs to the north-east to teach Sāṅkhya.

beats.title: Chapter 1
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (Canto 4, Chapter 1) narrates the genealogies of various sages and deities, beginning with Manu's daughters and their marriages to sages like Ruchi, Kardama, and Daksha. It details the births of important figures such as Yajna (an incarnation of Vishnu), the Tuṣita gods, the sons of Atri (Dattatreya, Durvasa, Soma), and the descendants of other sages like Angiras, Pulastya, Pulaha, Kratu, Vasishtha, Atharva, Bhrigu, and Daksha. The chapter also describes the birth of Nara-Narayana from Dharma and Murti, and concludes with the lineage of Agni and the Pitris.

beats.title: The Curse of Daksha and the Conflict with Shiva
beats.summary: Daksha, offended by Shiva not rising to greet him at a sacrificial assembly, curses Shiva to be excluded from Vedic sacrifices. In retaliation, Nandishvara curses Daksha and his followers to become heretics. Bhṛgu then curses Shiva's followers to become Pashandas. The chapter highlights the conflict between Daksha and Shiva, leading to mutual curses.

beats.title: Sati's Unheeded Warning
beats.summary: Sati, daughter of Daksha and wife of Shiva, desires to attend her father's grand sacrifice. Shiva advises against it, warning that Daksha despises him and will insult them. Sati insists, citing social norms, but Shiva predicts disaster. She goes anyway, leading to conflict.

beats.title: Sati's Self-Immolation
beats.summary: Sati, daughter of Daksha and wife of Shiva, attends Daksha's grand sacrifice uninvited. She is insulted by Daksha's neglect and his hostility toward Shiva. In a fury, she curses Daksha and immolates herself through yogic fire. Her attendants attack Daksha but are repelled by the Rbhus, celestial beings invoked by Bhrigu.

beats.title: Destruction of Daksha's Sacrifice
beats.summary: Rudra (Shiva) becomes furious upon learning from Narada that Daksha insulted his wife Sati at a sacrifice. He creates a fearsome being Virabhadra from his matted hair, who along with Rudra's attendants, destroys Daksha's sacrifice, attacks the priests and gods, and ultimately beheads Daksha. Rudra then departs.

beats.title: The Gods Seek Shiva's Forgiveness
beats.summary: The gods, defeated by Rudra's forces, approach Brahma for help. Brahma advises them to seek forgiveness from Shiva, who is angered by Daksha's sacrifice. The gods travel to Mount Kailasa, described in lush detail, and find Shiva in deep meditation. Brahma praises Shiva's supremacy and asks him to restore the sacrifice and heal the gods' injuries.

beats.title: The Completion of Daksha's Sacrifice and the Appearance of Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter describes the aftermath of Daksha's sacrifice, which was disrupted by Shiva. Daksha, having insulted Shiva, loses his head and is later restored with a goat's head. The gods and sages praise Vishnu, who appears and teaches about non-duality. The sacrifice is completed, and Sati, Daksha's daughter, is reborn as Parvati.

beats.title: The Story of Dhruva
beats.summary: Maitreya narrates the lineage of Svayambhuva Manu, focusing on Uttanapada and his two wives, Suniti and Suruci. Suruci's son Uttama is favored, while Suniti's son Dhruva is insulted by Suruci. Grieved, Dhruva resolves to attain the highest position. His mother advises him to worship Lord Vasudeva. Dhruva meets Narada, who tries to dissuade him but ultimately instructs him in bhakti-yoga, including meditation on the Lord's form and the mantra 'Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya'. Dhruva goes to Madhuvana and performs severe penance, controlling his breath and diet. His meditation becomes so intense that the worlds tremble, and the gods seek refuge in the Lord. The Lord assures them he will stop Dhruva.

beats.title: Dhruva's Return and Coronation
beats.summary: Dhruva returns to his kingdom after receiving a boon from Lord Vishnu. He is welcomed by his family and the citizens, and eventually becomes king. His father Uttānapāda retires to the forest.

beats.title: Dhruva's Battle with the Yakshas and the Asura Illusion
beats.summary: Dhruva, enraged by the killing of his brother Uttama by the Yakshas (Puṇya-jana), attacks their city in the Himalayas. He defeats the Yaksha army, but then faces a magical illusion created by the surviving Asuras, causing terrifying phenomena. The sages arrive and pray to Lord Vishnu for Dhruva's protection.

beats.title: Manu's Advice to Dhruva
beats.summary: Manu advises Dhruva to control his anger and seek the supreme Lord, explaining the nature of time, karma, and the self.

beats.title: Dhruva's Ascent to the Supreme Abode
beats.summary: Dhruva, after being pacified by Kubera, returns to his kingdom, rules for 36,000 years, then renounces everything and performs intense meditation on Vishnu. He is visited by Vishnu's attendants Sunanda and Nanda in a celestial chariot, and ascends to the supreme abode of Vishnu, becoming the pole star. The chapter concludes with Narada praising Dhruva's achievement and Maitreya describing the benefits of reciting this story.

beats.title: The Lineage from Dhruva to Prithu and the Story of King Vena
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the lineage from Dhruva to King Vena and the birth of Prithu. It begins with Vidura asking Maitreya about the Pracetas and the story of King Vena. Maitreya recounts how Dhruva's son Utkala renounced the throne, leading to a succession of kings. King Anga performed a sacrifice but the gods did not accept the offerings; advised by priests, he propitiated Vishnu and obtained a son, Vena, who became wicked. Anga abandoned the kingdom, and Vena's tyranny led the sages to kill him and later churn Prithu from his right arm.

beats.title: The Fall of King Vena and the Origin of the Niṣādas
beats.summary: The sages, seeing the wickedness of King Vena, first try to counsel him, but he arrogantly rejects their advice, claiming himself to be the supreme deity. The sages then curse him to death. After his death, chaos ensues, and the sages churn his thigh, producing a dwarf-like man who becomes the progenitor of the Niṣādas. The chapter highlights the dangers of pride and the necessity of righteous rule.

beats.title: The Birth and Coronation of King Pṛthu
beats.summary: King Pṛthu, born as an incarnation of Viṣṇu from the churning of King Vena's body, is crowned emperor. The sages and gods present him with divine gifts, and he humbly questions the bards' praise, emphasizing that true praise belongs to the Supreme Lord.

beats.title: The Bards' Praise of King Pṛthu
beats.summary: The bards, prompted by sages, praise King Pṛthu, describing him as an incarnation of Viṣṇu's power, a righteous ruler who protects dharma, subdues evil, and performs great deeds such as milking the earth and performing a hundred horse sacrifices. They extol his virtues and predict his future glory.

beats.title: Pṛthu Milks the Earth
beats.summary: King Pṛthu, angered by the earth's refusal to yield sustenance for his starving subjects, pursues her with a bow. The earth, in the form of a cow, flees but eventually submits, praising the Supreme Lord and reminding Pṛthu of his duty. She then agrees to be milked, providing all necessities.

beats.title: The Milking of the Earth by Pṛthu and All Beings
beats.summary: Earth (Bhūmi) approaches King Pṛthu in fear, explaining that she has withheld vegetation due to neglect by rulers. She advises Pṛthu to milk her using proper methods, and he does so, becoming the first king to establish agriculture and settlements. Various beings then milk Earth for their respective desires, demonstrating the principle of extracting essence from the world.

beats.title: Pṛthu's Horse Sacrifice and Indra's Obstruction
beats.summary: King Pṛthu performs a hundred horse sacrifices. Indra, envious, steals the sacrificial horse multiple times, taking on various disguises. Pṛthu's son pursues but spares Indra due to his ascetic appearance. Atri identifies Indra and urges the son to kill him, but Indra escapes. Pṛthu becomes angry and prepares to shoot Indra, but the priests advise against it and call Indra through Vedic hymns. Brahmā intervenes, explaining that Indra is not to be killed as the sacrifice is a form of the Lord. He reveals that Indra's actions created pseudo-religions (pākhaṇḍas) to mislead people. Pṛthu is advised to forgive Indra and destroy the false religions. Pṛthu reconciles with Indra, completes the sacrifice, and receives blessings from the sages.

beats.title: The Lord's Instructions to King Pṛthu
beats.summary: In this chapter, Lord Vishnu (Vaikuṇṭha) appears before King Pṛthu and Indra after the completion of a hundred horse sacrifices. Vishnu instructs Pṛthu on the nature of the self, detachment, and the path of devotion. Pṛthu refuses material boons, seeking only the nectar of Vishnu's lotus feet. Vishnu blesses him with unflinching devotion and advises him to rule righteously. The chapter concludes with the departure of Vishnu and the celestial beings, and Pṛthu returns to his capital.

beats.title: King Pṛthu's Rule and Teachings
beats.summary: King Pṛthu, after being crowned, rules righteously. He enters his city amidst grand celebrations, performs sacrifices, and addresses an assembly of sages and citizens, teaching about the duties of a king and the importance of serving the brāhmaṇas and the Supreme Lord. The chapter concludes with praise from the sages.

beats.title: Pṛthu's Enlightenment and Ideal Rule
beats.summary: King Pṛthu receives the four Kumāra sages, headed by Sanat-kumāra, who teach him the path of bhakti-yoga and detachment. Pṛthu expresses gratitude and offers everything to the sages. The sages depart, and Pṛthu, now enlightened, performs his duties with detachment, ruling as an ideal king and begetting five sons.

beats.title: The Liberation of King Pṛthu and Queen Arci
beats.summary: King Pṛthu, after ruling righteously, retires to the forest to perform severe austerities, following the yoga taught by Sanat-kumāra. He attains pure devotion to Kṛṣṇa, cuts off all attachments, and finally leaves his body through a systematic yogic process, merging the elements. His devoted wife Arci immolates herself on his pyre, and the celestial wives praise her. The chapter concludes with the glories of hearing this narrative.

beats.title: The Pracetās and the Hymn of Rudra
beats.summary: Maitreya narrates the lineage of King Pṛthu, focusing on his son Vijitāśva and subsequent descendants. The story culminates with the ten Pracetā sons who perform severe penance in the ocean, where they encounter Lord Śiva (Rudra). Śiva teaches them a hymn to the Supreme Lord (Vāsudeva) and instructs them in bhakti-yoga, emphasizing devotion over mere ritual.

beats.title: The Allegory of King Puranjana
beats.summary: Narada instructs King Pracinabarhi on the allegory of King Puranjana, who enters a nine-gated city and enjoys sense pleasures with his queen, illustrating the soul's entanglement in material life.

beats.title: The King's Hunting and Repentance
beats.summary: King Purañjana goes hunting, neglects his queen, and later regrets his actions, seeking her forgiveness. The chapter uses allegory to discuss karma, attachment, and the importance of the wife as a guiding force.

beats.title: The Fall of King Purañjana
beats.summary: King Purañjana, enamored by his queen Purañjanī, neglects his duties and becomes attached to family life. He performs violent sacrifices, and eventually time (Kāla) approaches in the form of Caṇḍavega, a Gandharva king, who attacks his city. Purañjana's protector Prajāgara fights for many years but eventually weakens. Meanwhile, Kālakanyā (Time's daughter) seeks a husband but is rejected by all, including Nārada. She finally marries Yavaneśvara (king of the Yavanas), who gives her an army to destroy Purañjana's city.

beats.title: The Fall and Liberation of King Purañjana
beats.summary: Narada narrates the allegory of King Purañjana (the embodied soul) whose city (the body) is attacked by the forces of time and death. After death, the soul becomes a woman (Vaidarbhī) and marries Malayadhvaja, who eventually renounces and attains liberation. A brahmana friend (the inner guru) awakens the soul to its true identity.

beats.title: The Allegory of the City of Nine Gates
beats.summary: Nārada explains the allegory of the city of nine gates (the body) and the king (the soul), describing the nature of the self, the senses, the mind, and the cycle of rebirth. He emphasizes that liberation comes through devotion to Vāsudeva (Kṛṣṇa) and hearing the Bhagavata Purāṇa. The chapter concludes with King Prācīnabarhi attaining liberation.

beats.title: The Penance of the Pracetas and the Birth of Dakṣa
beats.summary: The Pracetas (sons of King Barhiṣmat) perform severe penance in the ocean, pleasing Lord Hari. The Lord appears and grants them boons: they will have a son Dakṣa, marry Māriṣā, and eventually attain liberation. The Pracetas praise the Lord, and after He departs, they emerge from the ocean and, angered by trees covering the earth, emit fire and wind from their mouths. Brahmā intervenes, and the remaining trees offer Māriṣā as their daughter. The Pracetas marry her, and Dakṣa is born, who later becomes a Prajāpati.

beats.title: Narada's Instructions to the Pracetas
beats.summary: Narada instructs the Pracetas on the supreme importance of devotion to Hari, using analogies like watering the root of a tree and the sun's rays. The Pracetas attain liberation. Vidura hears this from Maitreya and departs for Hastinapura.

beats.title: The Story of King Priyavrata and His Lineage
beats.summary: This chapter of the Bhagavata Purana (Fifth Canto, Chapter 1) narrates the story of King Priyavrata, a great devotee of the Lord, who initially renounces the world but is persuaded by his father Manu and Lord Brahma to accept the responsibility of ruling the earth. It describes his marriage, his sons, and his eventual renunciation after fulfilling his duties. The chapter also introduces his son Agnidhra and the lineage leading to King Nabhi and his son Rishabha.

beats.title: The Appearance and Glories of Ṛṣabhadeva
beats.summary: This chapter describes the lineage and deeds of King Nābhi and his son Ṛṣabhadeva, an incarnation of the Lord. Ṛṣabhadeva performed many sacrifices, taught dharma by example, and had one hundred sons, including the eldest Bharata, after whom the land Bhārata-varṣa is named. The chapter also introduces nine great devotees who understood bhāgavata-dharma, and notes that Ṛṣabhadeva instructed his sons on the path of renunciation.

beats.title: The Teachings and Renunciation of Rishabha
beats.summary: Rishabha instructs his sons on the path of renunciation and devotion, emphasizing the futility of material pursuits and the importance of tapas, self-realization, and service to the guru. He then renounces the world, wandering as an avadhuta, performing extreme ascetic practices, and ultimately achieving liberation.

beats.title: The Life and Teachings of King Bharata
beats.summary: This chapter describes the life and teachings of King Bharata, a great devotee of Lord Vasudeva. It narrates his rule, his performance of Vedic sacrifices with devotion, his renunciation, and his intense bhakti-yoga practice at Pulaha Ashrama. The chapter also includes a prophecy about a future king who will be misled by Kali's influence and start a heretical path, and concludes with verses praising Bharata's exemplary conduct and the supreme value of bhakti over liberation.

beats.title: King Bharata's Meditation and Liberation
beats.summary: This chapter describes the meditation of King Bharata on the Supreme Lord as the cosmic being, and his attainment of liberation. It includes a hymn to the sun as a representation of the divine.

beats.title: The Fall and Redemption of King Bharata
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Bharata, who becomes attached to a deer and is reborn as a deer due to that attachment. In his next life, he is born as a brahmana but feigns madness to avoid worldly entanglements. He is later forced to carry a palanquin for King Rahugana, and through his teachings, he reveals his spiritual realization.

beats.title: The Humility of King Rahūgaṇa
beats.summary: A dialogue between King Rahūgaṇa and the avadhūta Jaḍa Bharata, who teaches the king about non-duality, detachment, and the futility of worldly distinctions. The king, humbled, seeks forgiveness and instruction.

beats.title: The Brāhmaṇa's Discourse on the Mind and Liberation
beats.summary: The brāhmaṇa explains that the mind, driven by the three guṇas, creates the cycle of saṃsāra. Liberation comes from detachment and devotion to the Supreme Lord, who is the inner witness (kṣetrajña). The mind's eleven functions (indriyas, prāṇa, etc.) bind the jīva, but by turning away from guṇas and surrendering to Hari, one attains freedom.

beats.title: The Instruction of Jaḍa Bharata to King Rahūgaṇa
beats.summary: King Rahūgaṇa, after being rebuked by the brāhmaṇa (Jaḍa Bharata) for his arrogance, humbly asks for instruction. The brāhmaṇa explains the illusory nature of the body and the world, the importance of detachment, and the path to liberation through devotion to Vāsudeva, recounting his own past life as King Bharata who became a deer due to attachment.

beats.title: The Allegory of the Soul's Journey through Saṃsāra
beats.summary: This chapter is an allegorical description of the journey of the jīva (individual soul) through the forest of saṃsāra (material existence), narrated by the brāhmaṇa to King Rahūgaṇa. It depicts the soul's struggles with the six sense enemies, attachment to family and possessions, and the ultimate goal of liberation through devotion to Hari and the grace of the guru.

beats.title: The Lineage of Bharata and the Glories of King Gaya
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (Canto 5, Chapter 14-15) narrates the lineage of King Bharata, highlighting his descendants including Sumati, Devatājin, Devadyumna, Parameṣṭhī, Pratīha, and the illustrious King Gaya. It describes Gaya as a great devotee of Viṣṇu who ruled righteously, embodying the principles of dharma and bhakti. The chapter also includes verses praising Bharata's renunciation and the glories of hearing his story.

beats.title: The Lineage of King Gaya
beats.summary: This chapter describes the lineage of King Gaya, a righteous and powerful ruler who performed grand sacrifices. It lists his descendants, including Citraratha, Samrat, Marici, Madhu, Viravrata, Manthu, Pramanthu, Bhauvana, Tvashta, Viraja, and others, culminating in a verse comparing Viraja's glory to that of Vishnu among the gods.

beats.title: Description of Jambudvīpa and the Celestial Ganges
beats.summary: This chapter describes the geography of Jambudvipa, centered on Mount Meru, including its nine varṣas (regions), eight boundary mountains, four supporting mountains with their trees, lakes, and gardens, and the rivers flowing from them. It also describes the celestial river Ganges descending from the sky, dividing into four streams, and the presence of Lord Nārāyaṇa in each varṣa, with special mention of Lord Śiva's worship in Ilāvṛta.

beats.title: Hymn of Praise to the Supreme Lord
beats.summary: This chapter is a hymn of praise to the Supreme Lord, describing His transcendence over material qualities and His role as the ultimate controller. It emphasizes that the Lord is beyond the grasp of the senses and mind, and that even great beings like Brahmā are dependent on His grace. The chapter also highlights the Lord's mercy in delivering souls from the cycle of karma.

beats.title: The Worship of the Lord's Incarnations in the Different Regions
beats.summary: This chapter describes the worship of the Supreme Lord in His various incarnations (Hayagriva, Narasimha, Kamadeva, Matsya, Kurma, Varaha) by the inhabitants of different regions (Bhadrashva, Hari, Ketumala, Ramyaka, Hiranmaya, and the northern Kurus). Each region's devotees chant specific mantras and offer prayers that highlight the Lord's transcendental qualities and pastimes.

beats.title: The Glories of Bharata and the Geography of Jambudvipa
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Kimpurusha region where Hanuman worships Rama, the Bharata region where Nara-Narayana perform penance, and lists the mountains, rivers, and subcontinents of Jambudvipa. It also discusses the nature of liberation and the superiority of human birth in Bharata for attaining devotion to Hari.

beats.title: The Islands of Bhū-maṇḍala and the Abode of the Lord
beats.summary: This chapter describes the seven concentric islands (dvīpas) and their surrounding oceans, each ruled by a son of Priyavrata. It details the geography, mountains, rivers, inhabitants, and their modes of worship of the Supreme Lord in various forms. The chapter culminates with the Lokāloka mountain and the abode of the Supreme Lord as the inner controller.

beats.title: The Celestial Order and the Sun's Chariot
beats.summary: This chapter describes the celestial geography and the motion of the sun, moon, planets, and stars around Mount Meru and Dhruva (the polestar). It details the sun's chariot, the divisions of time (days, nights, months, seasons, and years), the orbits of the planets, and the arrangement of the nakṣatras. The chapter concludes with the description of the śiśumāra (dolphin) form of Viṣṇu, which encompasses all celestial bodies, and a meditation on that form.

beats.title: The Celestial Deities and Sin Removal
beats.summary: This chapter describes the celestial arrangement of planets, stars, and constellations as the presiding deities of the universe. It states that worshiping or remembering them at the three junctures of the day (morning, noon, evening) destroys sins incurred during those times.

beats.title: Description of the Subterranean Realms and Hellish Planets
beats.summary: This chapter describes the lower planetary systems (bila-svarga) beneath the earth, including the seven subterranean realms (atala, vitala, sutala, talātala, mahātala, rasātala, pātāla), their inhabitants (daityas, dānavas, nāgas, etc.), and the glories of Lord Ananta (Sesa). It also details the hellish planets (naraka) where sinners suffer according to their deeds, and concludes with the description of the universal form of the Lord.

beats.title: Meditation on the Gross and Subtle Forms
beats.summary: This chapter describes the process of meditation on the gross and subtle forms of the Lord, guiding the yogi to withdraw the mind from the gross to the subtle, culminating in realization of the Supreme.

beats.title: The Deliverance of Ajāmila
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about the path of nivṛtti (renunciation) and pravṛtti (worldly activity), and how one can avoid hellish punishments. Śuka explains that mere prāyaścitta (atonement) is insufficient; true purification comes through devotion to Vāsudeva (Kṛṣṇa). He narrates the story of Ajāmila, a fallen brāhmaṇa who, at death, called out his son's name 'Nārāyaṇa' and was saved by Viṣṇudūtas from the messengers of Yamarāja. The Viṣṇudūtas debate with the Yamadūtas about the nature of dharma and adharma, emphasizing that sincere chanting of the Lord's name absolves all sins.

beats.title: The Deliverance of Ajamila
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of Ajamila, a fallen brahmana who, at the moment of death, calls out to his son Narayana, inadvertently uttering the holy name of Lord Vishnu. The Vishnudutas (messengers of Vishnu) rescue him from the Yamadutas (messengers of Yama), arguing that even an unintentional utterance of the Lord's name absolves all sins. Ajamila, freed, repents and goes to the Ganges to practice yoga, ultimately attaining liberation and reaching Vishnu's abode.

beats.title: Yama Instructs His Messengers
beats.summary: Yama explains to his messengers that the supreme authority is Lord Vishnu, not himself. He describes the power of chanting the Lord's names, citing Ajamila's liberation. Yama lists the twelve authorities on Bhagavata Dharma and instructs his servants to avoid those who are devoted to Krishna.

beats.title: The Birth of Dakṣa and His Penance
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about the secondary creation (anusarga) by Dakṣa. Śuka narrates how the ten Prācetasa sons, emerging from the ocean, became angry at the trees covering the earth and emitted fire from their mouths. King Soma (the Moon god) pacified them, advising them to control anger and accept a maiden (Māriṣā) as wife. They married her, and from her was born Dakṣa Prācetasa. Dakṣa then performed penance at Aghamarṣaṇa tīrtha, praised the Lord with the Haṃsa-guhya hymn, and was granted a wife (Asiknī) and the power to create progeny through sexual union.

beats.title: The Dissuasion of Daksha's Sons by Narada
beats.summary: Daksha, at Brahma's behest, first produces ten thousand Haryashva sons who are sent to create progeny. They encounter Narada, who dissuades them with riddles about the nature of the world, leading them to renounce and take the path of no return. Daksha then produces another thousand Savalashva sons, who also meet Narada and are similarly led away. Daksha curses Narada, but Narada accepts it calmly.

beats.title: Chapter 6
beats.summary: This chapter enumerates the progeny of Daksha's daughters, detailing the lineages of various deities, sages, demons, and other beings. It traces the descendants of Dharma, Bhuta, Angiras, Kṛśāśva, Tārkṣya, Soma, and Kaśyapa, including the Ādityas, Vasus, Rudras, Maruts, and others. The chapter also describes the marriages and offspring of Kaśyapa's wives, leading to the creation of diverse species.

beats.title: Indra's Fall and Redemption through Viśvarūpa
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka how the demons defeated the gods after Indra insulted his guru Bṛhaspati. Śuka narrates: Indra, intoxicated by power, failed to honor Bṛhaspati when he arrived. Bṛhaspati left, and the demons, under Śukrācārya's guidance, attacked the gods. The gods sought refuge in Brahmā, who advised them to approach Viśvarūpa, son of Tvaṣṭā, as their priest. Viśvarūpa accepted and, through the Viṣṇu-vidyā, restored Indra's prosperity and defeated the demons.

beats.title: The Nārāyaṇa-kavaca: The Armor of Nārāyaṇa
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about the Nārāyaṇa-kavaca, a protective hymn taught by Viśvarūpa to Indra. The hymn invokes various avatars and weapons of Viṣṇu for protection from all dangers. Śuka concludes by describing its power and the story of the sage Kauśika who used it.

beats.title: The Origin of Vṛtra and the Gods' Plea to Viṣṇu
beats.summary: The gods, terrified by the demon Vṛtra (created by Tvaṣṭā), seek refuge in Viṣṇu. Viṣṇu instructs them to obtain the bones of the sage Dadhyañca, which will be used to make a weapon to kill Vṛtra. The chapter also recounts the origin of Vṛtra from Tvaṣṭā's sacrifice and the distribution of Brahmahatyā (sin of killing a brāhmaṇa) among earth, trees, women, and water.

beats.title: The Battle Between Devas and Asuras: Dadhyaṅṅ's Sacrifice and Vṛtra's Speech
beats.summary: The chapter describes the battle between the devas and asuras, focusing on the sage Dadhyan (Dadhyaṅṅ Ātharvaṇa) who sacrifices his body for the devas, enabling Indra to obtain the vajra weapon. The asuras, led by Vṛtra, attack fiercely but are repelled by the devas. Vṛtra then delivers a speech on the inevitability of death and the glory of dying in battle.

beats.title: Vritra's Discourse and Death
beats.summary: Vritra, the demon king, rallies his fleeing army, rebukes them for cowardice, and single-handedly attacks the gods. He defeats Indra's elephant Airavata and then delivers a profound discourse on the futility of material desires and the supreme value of devotion to Vishnu, before urging Indra to strike him with the thunderbolt.

beats.title: The Battle of Indra and Vritra
beats.summary: Vritra, the demon king, fights Indra. Despite losing an arm, Vritra preaches about the supremacy of the Supreme Lord and the futility of ego. Indra, inspired, fights on and eventually kills Vritra, who attains liberation.

beats.title: Indra's Fall and Restoration
beats.summary: After Indra kills Vritra, he is afflicted by the sin of brahmahatya (killing a brahmana). The sages advise him to perform an Ashvamedha sacrifice to the Supreme Lord Narayana to purify himself. Indra flees from the sin personified as a hideous woman, hides in a lotus stalk for a thousand years, and during his absence, Nahusha rules heaven but falls due to pride. Eventually, the sages perform the horse sacrifice, and Indra is purified and restored.

beats.title: The Story of King Citraketu
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka how the demon Vṛtra, born of rajas and tamas, could have such firm devotion to Nārāyaṇa. Śuka replies by narrating the story of King Citraketu, who was childless despite many wives. The sage Aṅgirā visits him, and Citraketu expresses his sorrow. Aṅgirā performs a sacrifice and gives the remnants to Citraketu's chief queen Kṛtadyuti, who conceives a son. The other queens, jealous, poison the child. Citraketu and Kṛtadyuti lament bitterly. Aṅgirā and Nārada then arrive.

beats.title: The Consolation of King Citraketu
beats.summary: King Citraketu, grieving his son's death, is consoled by the sages Angira and Narada. They teach him about the illusory nature of the body and attachments, urging him to seek the Supreme Lord. Narada gives him a mantra to realize Sankarshana.

beats.title: The Liberation of Citraketu
beats.summary: Narada revives the dead son of King Citraketu and teaches him about the eternal nature of the soul. Citraketu, enlightened, performs penance, receives a mantra from Narada, and attains the vision of Lord Ananta (Sesha). He praises the Lord and receives further instruction on non-duality and devotion.

beats.title: The Curse of Citraketu and the Glory of Vishnu's Devotees
beats.summary: Citraketu, a Vidyadhara king, mocks Lord Shiva for publicly embracing his wife Parvati. Parvati curses him to become a demon. Citraketu accepts the curse with equanimity, teaching about the illusory nature of duality. Lord Shiva then explains to Parvati the greatness of Vishnu's devotees, who are beyond dualities. Citraketu is later born as the demon Vritra.

beats.title: The Birth of the Maruts
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the lineage of various beings from the creator deities, focusing on the progeny of Kashyapa and his wives, especially Diti. It describes the birth of the Maruts from Diti's womb, which was cut into seven pieces by Indra, but the children survived due to the grace of Vishnu. The story highlights Diti's vow to have a son to kill Indra, her observance of a year-long vow, Indra's attempt to find a flaw, and the eventual transformation of the Maruts into divine beings.

beats.title: The Puṃsavana Vow for Viṣṇu and Śrī
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Puṃsavana vow (vrata) dedicated to Lord Viṣṇu and Goddess Śrī, including its procedure, mantras, offerings, and the benefits for both men and women who observe it. The vow is performed over twelve months, culminating in a final ceremony. It emphasizes devotion, purity, and the grace of Viṣṇu and Śrī for fulfilling desires.

beats.title: The Story of Jaya and Vijaya: Liberation Through Enmity
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (Book 7, Chapter 1) begins with King Yudhishthira asking Sage Narada how the Lord, who is impartial, could kill the demons for Indra's sake, and how enemies like Shishupala and Dantavakra attained liberation. Narada explains that the Lord, though transcendent, acts through the modes of nature, and that even hatred and fear can lead to liberation if the mind is fixed on Krishna. He recounts the story of Jaya and Vijaya, Vishnu's gatekeepers, who were cursed by the Sananda Kumaras to be born as demons, and their subsequent births as Hiranyakashipu, Hiranyaksha, Ravana, Kumbhakarna, Shishupala, and Dantavakra, all of whom were killed by the Lord's avatars and thus returned to Vaikuntha.

beats.title: Hiraṇyakaśipu's Grief and Philosophical Discourse
beats.summary: Hiraṇyakaśipu, enraged by the death of his brother Hiraṇyākṣa at the hands of Viṣṇu (as the boar incarnation), orders the demons to devastate the earth, targeting brahmins, cows, and Vedic practices. The demons wreak havoc, causing the gods to flee. Hiraṇyakaśipu consoles his grieving family with a philosophical discourse on the nature of the self, using the story of King Suyajña and Yama's teaching to illustrate the illusion of attachment. The chapter ends with Diti and her daughters-in-law abandoning grief and focusing on truth.

beats.title: Hiraṇyakaśipu's Boon
beats.summary: Hiraṇyakaśipu performs severe penance to become immortal and invincible. The gods, distressed by his tapas, appeal to Brahmā, who grants Hiraṇyakaśipu a boon that makes him nearly impossible to kill, except under specific conditions.

beats.title: Hiranyakashipu's Boon and Oppression; Prahlada's Devotion
beats.summary: Brahma grants boons to Hiranyakashipu after his severe penance. Empowered, Hiranyakashipu conquers all worlds and usurps Indra's throne, oppressing the universe. The gods seek refuge in Vishnu, who assures them that He will kill Hiranyakashipu when he persecutes his own son Prahlada, a great devotee. Prahlada's virtues and ecstatic love for Krishna are described.

beats.title: The Gathering at Kurukshetra
beats.summary: This chapter describes the beginning of the Kurukshetra war, with Dhritarashtra asking Sanjaya about the activities of his sons and the Pandavas. Sanjaya narrates Duryodhana's address to Dronacharya, listing the prominent warriors on both sides, the blowing of conches, and the formation of armies.

beats.title: The Story of Prahlāda and Hiraṇyakaśipu
beats.summary: In this chapter, the sage Nārada recounts the story of Prahlāda and his father Hiraṇyakaśipu. The demon king Hiraṇyakaśipu, enraged by his son's devotion to Viṣṇu, attempts to kill Prahlāda through various means, but Prahlāda is protected by the Lord. The chapter explores themes of divine protection, the power of devotion, and the nature of true righteousness.

beats.title: Prahlāda's Unwavering Devotion
beats.summary: Hiraṇyakaśipu's son Prahlāda, despite being raised by demonic teachers, develops devotion to Viṣṇu. He teaches his father that true dharma is bhakti, not the three aims of life. Hiraṇyakaśipu tries to kill him through various means, but Prahlāda remains unharmed due to his devotion. The demon king becomes anxious, and his teachers advise binding Prahlāda until their return.

beats.title: Prahlada's Instructions to the Daitya Children
beats.summary: Prahlada instructs the Daitya children on the urgency of spiritual life, the futility of material pursuits, and the supremacy of devotion to Lord Vishnu. He emphasizes that human life is rare and should be used for attaining the Lord's lotus feet, not for transient pleasures. The chapter concludes with the Daitya children questioning how Prahlada, a child confined to the palace, gained such wisdom.

beats.title: Prahlada's Instruction to the Daitya Children
beats.summary: Narada narrates how Prahlada's mother was captured by Indra but rescued by Narada, and how Prahlada learned devotion from Narada while in the womb. Prahlada then teaches the Daitya children about the nature of the self, the futility of material pursuits, and the supreme importance of unalloyed devotion to Lord Vishnu.

beats.title: The Slaying of Hiranyakashipu by Narasimha
beats.summary: Narada narrates the story of Hiranyakashipu's attempt to kill his son Prahlada for his devotion to Vishnu. Hiranyakashipu challenges Prahlada's assertion that Vishnu is everywhere, and strikes a pillar, from which Narasimha (half-man, half-lion) emerges and kills Hiranyakashipu. Various celestial beings then offer prayers to Narasimha.

beats.title: Prahlada's Prayer and Narasimha's Grace
beats.summary: Prahlada, a devotee of Vishnu, is sent by the gods to pacify Narasimha after Hiranyakashipu's death. He offers a profound prayer, expressing humility, devotion, and philosophical insights about the nature of God and the path of bhakti. Narasimha, pleased, offers him a boon, but Prahlada refuses material desires, seeking only to serve the Lord.

beats.title: Prahlada's Devotion and the Fate of Jaya and Vijaya
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (Canto 7, Chapter 10) narrates the aftermath of Narasimha's killing of Hiranyakashipu. Prahlada refuses material boons from Lord Narasimha, emphasizing pure devotion without desire. The Lord instructs Prahlada to rule the demons and perform his father's funeral rites. Brahma praises the Lord, and the Lord explains that even enemies like Hiranyakashipu and Hiranyaksha, who were originally Vishnu's gatekeepers Jaya and Vijaya, attain liberation through constant enmity. The chapter concludes with the story of Jaya and Vijaya's three births as demons and their eventual return to Vishnu's abode.

beats.title: The Pacification of Lord Narasimha and the Blessing of Prahlada
beats.summary: This chapter describes the aftermath of Prahlada's victory over Hiranyakashipu. Lord Narasimha, having killed the demon king, is pacified by Prahlada's prayers. The Lord then blesses Prahlada and establishes him as the ruler of the daityas. The chapter highlights the power of devotion and the Lord's mercy.

beats.title: The Burning of Tripura
beats.summary: Narada narrates to King Yudhishthira the story of Tripura-daha, where Lord Shiva, empowered by Vishnu's strategy, burns the three cities of the demons. The chapter highlights the supremacy of Vishnu and the interplay of divine will.

beats.title: The Eternal Dharma for All Varṇas
beats.summary: Nārada explains the eternal dharma for all varṇas and āśramas, as heard from Nārāyaṇa, detailing duties for brāhmaṇas, kṣatriyas, vaiśyas, śūdras, women, and mixed castes, emphasizing gradual detachment through prescribed actions.

beats.title: Duties of Brahmacarya and Vānaprastha
beats.summary: Narada describes the duties and lifestyle of a brahmachari (celibate student) living in the guru's house, including daily routines, begging for alms, and celibacy rules. Then he outlines the vānaprastha (forest-dweller) stage, with its austerities, and finally the process of renunciation (sannyāsa) and meditation on the Supreme, culminating in liberation.

beats.title: The Dialogue Between Prahlada and the Avadhuta
beats.summary: Narada describes the conduct of a paramahamsa sannyasi, who renounces all possessions and wanders alone, seeing the world as illusion. He recounts a dialogue between Prahlada and an avadhuta brahmana (the sage Ajagara), who teaches detachment through the example of a python and a bee.

beats.title: Duties of a Householder
beats.summary: Narada instructs Yudhishthira on the duties of a householder, emphasizing detachment, worship of Krishna through all actions, charity, and observance of sacred times and places. The chapter highlights that the ultimate recipient of all offerings is the Supreme Lord, and that the brahmana is the highest receptacle for worship.

beats.title: Narada's Instructions on Dharma and the Path to Liberation
beats.summary: Narada instructs King Yudhishthira on dharma, detailing proper conduct for householders, the pitfalls of desire and greed, the path of yoga and self-control, the distinction between pravritti and nivritti karma, and the means to attain liberation through devotion to Krishna. He concludes by recounting his own past life as a Gandharva cursed to become a Shudra, and his eventual rebirth as a sage through service.

beats.title: The Manvantaras and Avatars of Hari
beats.summary: This chapter describes the first Manvantara (Svāyambhuva) and subsequent Manvantaras up to the fourth (Tāmasa). It recounts the lineage of Manus, the appearance of Lord Hari as Yajña, Vibhu, Satyasena, and Hari (son of Harimedhas), and the liberation of Gajendra. The chapter also includes teachings on the nature of the Supreme Being and the path of detachment.

beats.title: Gajendra's Deliverance
beats.summary: This chapter describes the beautiful Trikuta mountain and its surroundings, including a divine garden and lake. The story focuses on Gajendra, the king of elephants, who, while bathing in the lake with his herd, is attacked by a powerful crocodile. After a long struggle, Gajendra realizes his helplessness and surrenders to the Supreme Lord, seeking protection.

beats.title: Gajendra's Deliverance
beats.summary: Gajendra, the king of elephants, is caught by a crocodile in a lake. After a long struggle, he prays to the Supreme Lord, offering a profound hymn of surrender and praise. The Lord, Hari, appears on Garuda and rescues Gajendra by killing the crocodile.

beats.title: Liberation of Gajendra
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Gajendra (the king of elephants) and his liberation by Lord Vishnu. It describes how Gajendra, formerly King Indradyumna, was cursed by sage Agastya to become an elephant due to a lapse in hospitality. Despite his elephant birth, he retained devotion to Vishnu. When caught by a crocodile (the gandharva Huhu under a curse), he prayed to Vishnu, who rescued him. Vishnu then revealed his divine forms and instructed on the practice of remembering him at dawn for liberation.

beats.title: The Fifth and Sixth Manvantaras and the Churning of the Ocean
beats.summary: This chapter describes the fifth manvantara (Raivata) and the sixth (Cākṣuṣa), focusing on the churning of the ocean by the gods and demons, and Brahmā's prayer to the Supreme Lord Viṣṇu for protection.

beats.title: The Churning of the Ocean
beats.summary: The gods, led by Brahma and Shiva, praise Lord Vishnu, who appears before them. Vishnu advises them to make peace with the demons and churn the ocean of milk for amrita, promising his help. The gods and demons agree, but when they try to lift Mount Mandara, they fail and are crushed. Vishnu then mounts Garuda, lifts the mountain, and carries it to the ocean, reviving the injured.

beats.title: The Churning of the Ocean
beats.summary: The chapter describes the churning of the ocean by the devas and asuras using Mount Mandara as the churning rod and Vasuki as the rope. Lord Hari (Vishnu) takes the lead, and the devas follow.

beats.title: The Gathering at Kurukshetra
beats.summary: The chapter describes the gathering of armies at Kurukshetra, with Dhritarashtra asking Sanjaya about the activities of his sons and the Pandavas. Sanjaya narrates Duryodhana's address to Dronacharya, listing prominent warriors on both sides, the blowing of conches, and the beginning of the battle.

beats.title: The Churning of the Ocean and Shiva Drinking the Poison
beats.summary: The devas and asuras churn the ocean of milk for amrita. The mountain Mandara sinks, and Vishnu as a tortoise supports it. Poison emerges, threatening the universe. The gods pray to Shiva, who compassionately consumes the poison, holding it in his throat, which turns blue. The chapter highlights Shiva's self-sacrifice and the virtue of protecting the distressed.

beats.title: The Churning of the Ocean and the Appearance of Lakshmi
beats.summary: The churning of the ocean yields various beings and objects, including the goddess Lakshmi, who chooses Vishnu as her eternal abode. The asuras seize the amrita pot, leading to conflict. Vishnu assumes the Mohini form to distract the asuras.

beats.title: The Churning of the Ocean: Distribution of Amrita
beats.summary: The chapter describes the churning of the ocean and the distribution of amrita. The asuras and devas, having churned the ocean, quarrel over the pot of nectar. Lord Vishnu, as the enchanting Mohini, appears and tricks the asuras into giving her the pot, then distributes the nectar only to the devas. The asura Rahu (Svarbhanu) disguises himself as a deva and drinks some nectar, but his head is cut off by Vishnu's discus. The chapter concludes with a philosophical reflection on the difference between those who seek the Lord's feet and those who do not.

beats.title: The Battle Between Devas and Asuras
beats.summary: The chapter describes the battle between the devas and asuras after the churning of the ocean. The asuras, led by Bali, attack the devas who have drunk the amrita. A fierce war ensues with various weapons and magical illusions. When the devas are overwhelmed, Lord Vishnu appears on Garuda and defeats the asura leaders, including Kalanemi, Mali, Sumali, and Malyavan.

beats.title: The Battle of the Gods and Demons: Indra's Victory and Narada's Intervention
beats.summary: The chapter describes the battle between the gods (devas) and the demons (asuras), focusing on Indra's fight against the demon Bali and his allies. After a fierce combat, Indra kills several demons including Jambha, Bala, Paka, and Namuci, using his vajra. The sage Narada, sent by Brahma, intervenes to stop the slaughter, and the surviving demons retreat with Bali's body. Shukracharya revives the fallen demons using his knowledge.

beats.title: Shiva's Vision of Mohini
beats.summary: Shiva, after hearing of Vishnu's Mohini avatar, becomes curious and asks to see it. Vishnu appears as a beautiful woman playing ball, captivating Shiva. Shiva pursues her, loses his seed, and later realizes the divine play. Vishnu reveals that his Maya is hard to overcome, and Shiva, having done so, is praised.

beats.title: The Fourteen Manvantaras
beats.summary: Śrī Śuka describes the fourteen Manvantaras, listing the Manus, their sons, the devas, Indras, saptarṣis, and the avatāras of Viṣṇu in each. The chapter covers past, present, and future Manvantaras, with emphasis on the current seventh (Vaivasvata) and the upcoming eighth (Sāvarṇi) through fourteenth (Indrasāvarṇi).

beats.title: The Order of Manvantaras and the Manifestations of Hari
beats.summary: The chapter explains the roles of Manus, Manu-putras, sages, Indras, and other beings in each manvantara, all acting under the direction of the Supreme Lord Hari. It describes how Hari manifests in various forms (e.g., as ṛṣi, yogeśa, prajeśa, kāla) to guide creation, maintain dharma, and ultimately dissolve the universe. The chapter concludes by stating that the fourteen manvantaras are the measure of a kalpa.

beats.title: Bali's Conquest of Heaven and the Bhṛgus' Boon
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka why Viṣṇu, as Vāmana, begged three paces of land from Bali and then bound him despite being the lord of sacrifice and complete. Śuka narrates how Bali, after being defeated by Indra and revived by the Bhṛgus, performed a Viśvajit sacrifice to conquer heaven. Equipped with divine weapons and a chariot from the Bhṛgus, Bali marched on Indra's capital. Indra, alarmed, consulted his guru Bṛhaspati, who explained that Bali's power came from the Bhṛgus' boon and advised the gods to temporarily abandon heaven. The gods did so, and Bali took over the three worlds. The Bhṛgus then performed a hundred horse sacrifices for Bali, who enjoyed great prosperity.

beats.title: Aditi's Lament and the Payovrata Vow
beats.summary: Aditi, distressed by the loss of her sons and the usurpation of heaven by the demons, is consoled by her husband Kaśyapa. He instructs her in the performance of the Payovrata vow to propitiate Lord Viṣṇu, detailing the rituals, mantras, and observances for twelve days. The vow is described as the essence of all sacrifices, penances, and gifts, and is sure to fulfill her desires.

beats.title: Aditi's Vow and Vishnu's Promise
beats.summary: Aditi performs the payovrata vow for twelve days, pleasing Lord Vishnu. He appears and promises to incarnate as her son to protect the gods. Aditi then approaches her husband Kashyapa, who, through yoga, understands the Lord's presence and impregnates her. Brahma praises the unborn child as the Supreme Lord.

beats.title: The Advent of Lord Vamana
beats.summary: This chapter describes the birth of Lord Vamana as the son of Aditi and Kashyapa, his appearance as a dwarf brahmana, his upanayana ceremony, and his journey to the sacrificial arena of King Bali. The sages and gods celebrate his birth, and Bali welcomes him with reverence.

beats.title: Bali Maharaja's Charity to Vamana
beats.summary: Bali Maharaja, the son of Virocana, offers land to Vamana (an avatar of Vishnu) as charity. Shukracarya warns Bali that Vamana is Vishnu in disguise and will take everything. Bali insists on keeping his promise, leading to the famous three-step request.

beats.title: Bali's Gift and Vamana's Cosmic Expansion
beats.summary: Bali Maharaja, despite being cursed by his guru Shukracharya for his refusal to break his promise, gives the three paces of land requested by Vamana (an avatar of Vishnu). Vamana then expands to cosmic proportions, covering the earth, sky, and heavens in two steps, and placing the third step on Bali's head, pushing him to the netherworlds. The chapter highlights Bali's unwavering truthfulness and devotion, and the cosmic vision of Vishnu's universal form.

beats.title: Vamana Binds Bali
beats.summary: Brahma and other gods worship Vamana, who then binds Bali with Varuna's ropes after Bali fails to fulfill his promise of three paces of land. Bali's followers attack but are restrained by Vishnu's attendants. Bali accepts his fate, recognizing the supremacy of time and divine will.

beats.title: Bali's Surrender and the Lord's Grace
beats.summary: Bali, bound by Varuna's ropes, remains unperturbed and speaks of his reverence for the Lord's punishment. Prahlada arrives, praises the Lord, and Bali's wife Vindhyavali pleads for his release. Brahma also intercedes. The Lord explains that He humbles the proud and grants grace to the humble, and declares that Bali will rule Sutala and become Indra in the future.

beats.title: Bali Maharaja Enters Sutala and the Glorification of Lord Vamana
beats.summary: Bali Maharaja, having been granted a boon by Lord Vamana, enters Sutala with the permission of the Lord. Prahlada praises the Lord's mercy. The Lord instructs Prahlada to go to Sutala. Later, Lord Hari asks Shukracharya to rectify any defects in Bali's sacrifice. Shukracharya praises the Lord and complies. Lord Vamana gives the three worlds to Indra and is installed as Upendra, the lord of all. All beings rejoice, and Indra, with the Lord's protection, enjoys the three worlds. The chapter concludes with the glory of hearing the Lord's pastimes.

beats.title: The Matsya Avatāra: The Fish Incarnation
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about the first avatāra of Hari, the Matsya (fish) avatāra. Śuka narrates how at the end of the previous kalpa, the demon Hayagrīva stole the Vedas from Brahmā. To retrieve them, Hari took the form of a fish. The fish approached King Satyavrata, who was performing water oblations, and asked for protection. The fish grew rapidly, revealing itself as the Lord. It instructed Satyavrata to board a boat with the seven sages, seeds, and herbs during the impending deluge, and to tie the boat to its horn. The Lord then taught him the supreme knowledge. After the deluge, Hari killed Hayagrīva and restored the Vedas. Satyavrata later became Vaivasvata Manu.

beats.title: The Story of King Sudyumna and the Curse of the Forest
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Sudyumna, who was born as a daughter named Ila due to a ritual mistake, then transformed into a male by Lord Shiva's boon, and later became a woman again upon entering a cursed forest. It details his marriage to Budha and the birth of Pururavas, and his eventual return to manhood through the grace of Vasistha and Shiva.
