beats.title: The Lineage of Manu Vaivasvata: Pṛṣadhra and the Dynasties of Nariṣyanta and Diṣṭa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (9th Skandha, Chapter 2) narrates the lineage of Manu Vaivasvata's sons, focusing on the stories of Pṛṣadhra and Kavi, and the dynasties of Nariṣyanta and Diṣṭa. It includes the curse of Pṛṣadhra by his guru, his subsequent penance and liberation, and the genealogies of various kings leading to Viśāla and the founding of Vaiśālī.

beats.title: The Marriage of Sukanyā and Cyavana, and the Rejuvenation
beats.summary: King Śaryāti's daughter Sukanyā accidentally blinds the sage Cyavana, who is then given to her in marriage. She serves him devotedly. The Aśvins rejuvenate Cyavana, and he grants them a share of the Soma sacrifice, angering Indra. Cyavana's power subdues Indra. Later, Śaryāti's descendant Kakudmī takes his daughter Revatī to Brahmā to seek a husband, but time has passed; Brahmā tells him to give her to Baladeva, an avatar of Viṣṇu. Kakudmī does so and retires to Badarī.

beats.title: The Protection of King Ambarīṣa and the Humility of Durvāsā
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Ambarīṣa, a great devotee of Viṣṇu, who performed the Dvādaśī vow. The sage Durvāsā, offended by a perceived slight, created a demon to kill the king, but the Sudarśana cakra of Viṣṇu protected Ambarīṣa and chased Durvāsā. Durvāsā sought refuge from Brahmā and Śiva, but they directed him to Viṣṇu, who taught that He is dependent on His devotees and advised Durvāsā to seek forgiveness from Ambarīṣa.

beats.title: The Protection of Ambarīṣa by Sudarśana Cakra
beats.summary: King Ambarīṣa, a devotee of Viṣṇu, is tested by the sage Durvāsā who, after being insulted, attacks him with a demon. Ambarīṣa prays to the Sudarśana cakra, which protects him and burns Durvāsā. Ambarīṣa then prays for the sage's well-being, and the cakra spares him. Durvāsā, humbled, praises Ambarīṣa's devotion and leaves. Ambarīṣa later renounces his kingdom and retires to the forest, focusing his mind on Vāsudeva.

beats.title: The Solar Dynasty: From Ikshvaku to Mandhata and the Tale of Saubhari
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (9th Skandha, Chapter 6) traces the solar dynasty (Suryavansha) from Manu through Ikshvaku and his descendants, including notable kings like Puranjaya (also known as Indravaha and Kakutstha), Mandhata, and the sage Saubhari's marriage to fifty princesses. It covers genealogies, battles with demons, and the story of Saubhari's renunciation after realizing the futility of sensual pleasures.

beats.title: The Story of Hariścandra and Śunaḥśepha
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the lineage of King Ambarīṣa and the story of Hariścandra, who, due to his desire for a son, makes a vow to Varuṇa to sacrifice his son. Through various delays and the intervention of Indra, the sage Viśvāmitra, and the purchase of Śunaḥśepha as a substitute, Hariścandra ultimately performs the Puruṣamedha sacrifice and attains liberation. The chapter also describes the spiritual ascent of Śunaḥśepha through meditation.

beats.title: The Saga of King Sagara and the Descent of the Ganga
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the lineage of King Sagar, his sons' destruction by Kapila's anger, and the subsequent efforts of his grandson Amshuman to retrieve the sacrificial horse and seek liberation for his ancestors. It includes the story of Asamanja, Amshuman's father, who was exiled but later revealed his yogic powers. The chapter culminates in Kapila's instruction to Amshuman that the ancestors require the waters of the Ganga for salvation.

beats.title: The Descent of the Ganges and the Lineage of the Sun Dynasty
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the descent of the Ganges to earth through the efforts of King Bhagiratha, the story of King Sudasa (Mitrasaha) who was cursed to become a rakshasa, and the lineage of the Ikshvaku dynasty culminating in King Khatvanga who attained liberation through devotion to Vasudeva.

beats.title: The Story of Lord Rama
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Lord Rama, an avatar of Vishnu, from his birth to his return to Ayodhya after defeating Ravana. It covers his marriage to Sita, exile, battles with demons, alliance with Sugriva and Hanuman, building of the bridge to Lanka, killing of Ravana, reunion with Sita, and coronation. The chapter emphasizes Rama's adherence to dharma and the ideal rule that brought prosperity.

beats.title: Rama's Later Life: Sacrifice, Abandonment of Sita, and Ascension
beats.summary: Rama performs a grand sacrifice, distributing directions to priests. He then hears gossip about Sita, abandons her despite her pregnancy, and she gives birth to twins Kuśa and Lava in Vālmīki's hermitage. Rama's brothers have sons and conquer territories. Sita enters the earth. Rama grieves but continues ascetic practices, ultimately attaining liberation.

beats.title: The Gathering at Kurukshetra
beats.summary: This chapter describes the beginning of the Kurukshetra war, with Dhritarashtra asking Sanjaya about the activities of his sons and the Pandavas. Sanjaya narrates Duryodhana's address to Dronacharya, listing the prominent warriors on both sides, and the blowing of conches signaling the start of battle.

beats.title: Rāma's Return and Reign in Ayodhyā
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śukadeva about Rāma's life after returning to Ayodhyā. Śukadeva describes Rāma's entry into the city, the joyous reception by citizens, and his peaceful reign with Sītā, enjoying royal pleasures while upholding dharma.

beats.title: The Future Kings of the Ikshvaku Dynasty
beats.summary: This chapter lists the future kings of the Ikshvaku dynasty from Kuśa's son Atithi down to Sumitra, who will be the last king before the dynasty ends in Kali Yuga. It also mentions the sage Maru who attained yoga-siddhi and will revive the solar dynasty at the end of Kali.

beats.title: The Story of King Nimi and the Mithila Dynasty
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Nimi, who curses his guru Vasiṣṭha for prioritizing Indra's sacrifice over his own, and receives a counter-curse. Nimi relinquishes his body, and the sages churn his body to produce a son named Janaka (also known as Vaideha and Mithila). The chapter then lists the subsequent kings of the Mithila dynasty, many of whom are described as knowers of the Self (ātmavidyāviśāradāḥ) and liberated even while living in households.

beats.title: The Lineage of Soma and the Tale of Pururavas and Urvashi
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the lineage of Soma (the moon god), including his abduction of Tara, the birth of Budha, and the story of Pururavas and Urvashi. It describes Pururavas' love for Urvashi, their separation due to a curse, and his eventual performance of a fire sacrifice to attain the Gandharva realm.

beats.title: The Lineage of Aila and the Wrath of Parashurama
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the lineage of King Aila (Pururavas) through his sons, leading to the birth of Jamadagni and his son Parashurama. It describes the arrogance of King Kartavirya Arjuna, who steals Jamadagni's wish-fulfilling cow, and Parashurama's subsequent wrath, leading to the killing of Kartavirya and the destruction of the Haihaya army. Jamadagni rebukes Parashurama for killing a king, emphasizing forgiveness.

beats.title: Parashurama's Vengeance and Vishvamitra's Lineage
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Parashurama, an avatar of Vishnu, who beheads his mother Renuka and his brothers at his father Jamadagni's command, later revives them. In revenge for his father's murder by Kartavirya Arjuna's sons, Parashurama exterminates the Kshatriyas twenty-one times, performs a sacrifice, and restores his father. The chapter also covers the lineage of Vishvamitra and his sons, including the adoption of Shunahshepha.

beats.title: The Lineages of Kshatravriddha and Anena
beats.summary: This chapter traces the lineage of King Pururavas through his son Ayus, focusing on the Kshatravriddha and Anena branches. It lists kings such as Kashi, Divodasa, Pratardana, and Alarka, and includes the story of Raji's sons who helped Indra defeat the demons but later refused to return heaven, leading to their destruction by Indra. The chapter also mentions Dhanvantari as an incarnation of Vasudeva and the origin of Ayurveda.

beats.title: The Story of Yayāti and His Sons
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Yayāti, his marriage to Devayānī and Śarmiṣṭhā, and the subsequent events leading to Yayāti's curse of premature old age and his exchange of youth with his son Pūru. It also describes Yayāti's eventual renunciation and worship of Vāsudeva.

beats.title: Yayāti's Renunciation
beats.summary: King Yayāti, after indulging in sensual pleasures for many years, becomes disillusioned and recounts a parable of a goat and a she-goat to illustrate the insatiable nature of desire. He renounces his kingdom, divides it among his sons, and retires to the forest to attain liberation. His wife Devayānī also hears the story and attains realization.

beats.title: The Lineage of Pūru and the Birth of Bharata and Bharadvāja
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the lineage of King Pūru, leading to Bharata, son of Duṣmanta and Śakuntalā. It describes Duṣmanta's encounter with Śakuntalā, their gāndharva marriage, the birth of Bharata, Duṣmanta's initial denial, and the divine voice affirming Bharata's legitimacy. Bharata's glorious reign, conquests, and eventual retirement are detailed, along with the crisis of his childless succession and the birth of Bharadvāja through divine intervention.

beats.title: The Compassion of Rantideva and the Lineage of Kings
beats.summary: This chapter recounts the lineage of King Rantideva and his exemplary compassion. Despite extreme poverty and hunger, Rantideva selflessly shared his meager food and water with successive guests—a brāhmaṇa, a śūdra, a man with dogs, and a pulkasa—until he had nothing left. His unwavering devotion to Hari and his prayer to bear others' suffering rather than seek liberation pleased the gods, who appeared before him. He remained detached and fixed his mind on Vāsudeva, causing māyā to vanish. The chapter then continues the dynastic genealogy from Vitatha through many kings and sages, including the birth of Kṛpa and Kṛpī.

beats.title: Chapter 22
beats.summary: This chapter traces the lineage of the Lunar dynasty (Somavaṃśa) from Mitrāyu through the Pāṇḍavas and Kauravas, culminating in the future kings of the Kali age, including the Magadha rulers. It highlights key figures such as Bhīṣma, Vyāsa, and the births of Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Pāṇḍu, and Vidura, as well as the Pāṇḍava and Kaurava descendants.

beats.title: Chapter 23
beats.summary: This chapter traces the dynastic lineages from Anu, including the Yadu, Turvasu, Druhyu, and Anu lines. It details the birth of kings, the story of Karna's adoption, the glory of Kartavirya Arjuna, and the lineage of the Yadus leading to the Vrishnis and the birth of Vidarbha through a miraculous promise.

beats.title: Chapter 24
beats.summary: This chapter traces the genealogy of the Yadu dynasty, focusing on the lineage of Vasudeva and Devaki, culminating in the birth of Krishna (Hari) as the eighth son. It describes the descendants of Vidarbha, the Satvata clan, and the various branches including the Vrishnis, Bhojas, and Andhakas. The chapter also narrates the story of Kunti (Pritha) and her son Karna, and lists the marriages and offspring of Vasudeva with his multiple wives. It concludes with a theological discourse on Krishna's divine descent to relieve the earth's burden and his pastimes.

beats.title: The Descent of Viṣṇu and the Birth of Kṛṣṇa
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about the descent of Viṣṇu in the Yadu dynasty, specifically about Kṛṣṇa's life. Śuka narrates how Earth, oppressed by demons, sought help from Brahmā, who along with other gods approached Viṣṇu. Viṣṇu promised to incarnate as Kṛṣṇa and Saṅkarṣaṇa. The story then details the marriage of Vasudeva and Devakī, the prophecy that Devakī's eighth son would kill Kaṃsa, Kaṃsa's attempts to kill the children, and the birth of Kṛṣṇa's previous six sons who were transferred to Rohiṇī. The chapter ends with Kaṃsa imprisoning Vasudeva and Devakī and killing their children out of fear.

beats.title: The Birth of Krishna and Balarama
beats.summary: This chapter describes the events leading to the birth of Krishna and Balarama. Kamsa, after killing six sons of Devaki, is unable to kill the seventh (Balarama) who is transferred to Rohini's womb. The eighth child, Krishna, is born. The gods praise Vishnu for his descent to relieve the earth's burden.

beats.title: The Birth of Krishna
beats.summary: This chapter describes the birth of Krishna (Vishnu) to Devaki and Vasudeva in the prison of Kamsa. The divine child appears with four arms and celestial ornaments, then transforms into an ordinary baby. Vasudeva praises him, and Devaki prays for protection. Krishna recounts their previous births as Prishni and Sutapa, and as Aditi and Kashyapa (Vamana). He then instructs Vasudeva to exchange the baby with Yogamaya, the newborn daughter of Yashoda. Vasudeva carries Krishna to Gokula, and the prison doors open miraculously. He returns with the baby girl, and Kamsa's plans are foiled.

beats.title: The Birth of Yogamaya and Kamsa's Remorse
beats.summary: Kamsa, informed by guards of Devaki's childbirth, rushes to the prison. Devaki pleads for her newborn daughter's life, but Kamsa seizes the baby and dashes her against a stone. The infant transforms into the goddess Yogamaya, who declares that Kamsa's killer has already been born elsewhere. She then vanishes. Kamsa, remorseful, releases Devaki and Vasudeva, but later, advised by his demon ministers, orders a massacre of infants and harassment of sages and cows to destroy the root of his enemy, Vishnu.

beats.title: Nanda's Celebration and Vasudeva's Visit
beats.summary: Nanda celebrates the birth of his son Krishna with great festivities in Vraja, including rituals, gifts, and music. Vasudeva visits Nanda in Mathura, expressing joy and philosophical reflections on life's transience. He warns of dangers in Gokula and advises Nanda to return quickly.

beats.title: The Killing of Putana
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the episode of the demoness Putana, sent by Kamsa to kill infant Krishna. She assumes a beautiful form, enters Nanda's house in Gokula, and attempts to breastfeed Krishna with poisoned milk. Krishna, however, sucks her life force along with her milk, killing her. Her gigantic corpse falls and devastates the surroundings. The Gopis perform protective rituals for Krishna. Nanda and the cowherds return from Mathura, see Putana's body, and are amazed. They cut it into pieces and burn it. The smoke from the burning body emits a fragrant scent, indicating Putana's purification. The chapter concludes by glorifying Putana's liberation and the power of devotion to Krishna.

beats.title: Childhood Pastimes of Krishna: Cart Overturning and Killing of Tṛṇāvarta
beats.summary: This chapter describes several childhood pastimes of Lord Krishna in Vrindavan, including the overturning of a cart (śakaṭa-bhañjana), the killing of the demon Tṛṇāvarta, and Yaśodā's vision of the universal form within Krishna's mouth. The events highlight Krishna's divine nature and the protection of his devotees.

beats.title: The Naming Ceremony and Childhood Pastimes of Krishna
beats.summary: Garga performs the naming ceremony for Krishna and Balarama in secret, revealing their divine identities. Krishna's childhood pastimes in Vrindavan are described, including his eating dirt and showing the universe in his mouth to mother Yashoda. The chapter concludes with the revelation that Nanda and Yashoda are incarnations of Vasu Drona and his wife Dhara, who prayed for parental devotion to the Lord.

beats.title: The Binding of Krishna and Liberation of the Yamala-Arjuna Trees
beats.summary: This chapter describes the pastime of Krishna breaking the churn and stealing butter, being caught by mother Yashoda, and her attempt to bind Him with ropes that always fall short. It also includes the story of Nalakuvara and Manigriva, who were cursed to become trees and later liberated by Krishna.

beats.title: The Liberation of Nalakūvara and Maṇigrīva
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about the curse on Nalakūvara and Maṇigrīva, the sons of Kubera. Śuka narrates how they became intoxicated with wealth and pride, disrespected the sage Nārada, and were cursed to become twin arjuna trees. Nārada's curse was actually a blessing in disguise, as they would later be liberated by Lord Kṛṣṇa. Kṛṣṇa, as a child, uprooted the mortar tied to his waist, causing the trees to fall and release the two beings, who then praised Kṛṣṇa and returned to their celestial abode.

beats.title: Childhood Pastimes in Vrindavan: The Move and Demon Slayings
beats.summary: This chapter describes Krishna and Balarama's childhood pastimes in Vrindavan, including the felling of the Yamala-Arjuna trees, the fruit vendor episode, the move from Gokula to Vrindavan due to portents, and the slaying of the demons Vatsasura and Bakasura.

beats.title: The Killing of Aghasura
beats.summary: Krishna and the cowherd boys go to the forest to play. The demon Agha, sent by Kamsa, takes the form of a huge python to devour them. Krishna enters the demon's mouth, expands his body, and kills him from within, freeing the boys. The gods rejoice, and the demon's body becomes a play cave. Later, King Parikshit asks about the timeline of this event.

beats.title: The Bewilderment of Brahma
beats.summary: Brahma tests Krishna by stealing the cowherd boys and calves, but Krishna expands himself to replace them all. Brahma is bewildered and eventually realizes Krishna's supreme divinity, bowing down in surrender.

beats.title: Brahma's Prayer and Krishna's Return
beats.summary: Brahma praises Krishna's divine form and pastimes, confesses his own ignorance, and extols the supreme fortune of the Vrajavasis. Krishna returns to Vrindavan with the cowherd boys after slaying the demon Aghasura. The chapter concludes with a discourse on the self as the dearest object and Krishna as the Supreme Self.

beats.title: Krishna and Balarama's Pastimes in Vrindavan: Killing of Dhenuka and Revival of the Dead
beats.summary: Krishna and Balarama, now in their early youth, enjoy pastimes in Vrindavan with the cowherd boys. They playfully interact with nature and animals. The gopas request them to fetch fruits from a talavana (palm grove) guarded by the demon Dhenuka. Balarama kills Dhenuka and his associates, freeing the grove. Later, Krishna revives the cows and gopas who died from drinking poisoned water from the Yamuna.

beats.title: Krishna Subdues Kaliya
beats.summary: Krishna subdues the serpent Kaliya in the Yamuna river, dances on his hoods, and after the serpent's wives pray for mercy, Krishna spares him and orders him to leave the river. The Yamuna becomes purified.

beats.title: Kāliya's Exile and Kṛṣṇa's Protection
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka how Kāliya left Ramaṇaka island and why Garuḍa did not kill him. Śuka explains that Kāliya, intoxicated by poison, usurped the offerings meant for Garuḍa. Garuḍa attacked but Kāliya fled to a lake in the Yamunā that was cursed by the sage Saubhari to kill any snake entering it. Kāliya lived there until Kṛṣṇa drove him out. After Kṛṣṇa emerged from the lake, the cowherds rejoiced. Nanda gave gifts to brāhmaṇas. That night, a forest fire threatened the camp, but Kṛṣṇa swallowed it, saving everyone.

beats.title: The Killing of Pralamba
beats.summary: Krishna and Balarama, along with the cowherd boys, engage in playful pastimes in the Vrindavana forest during summer, which feels like spring due to the divine presence. The demon Pralamba, disguised as a cowherd, attempts to abduct Balarama but is killed by him. The gods celebrate Balarama's victory.

beats.title: The Forest Fire Rescue
beats.summary: Krishna and Balarama rescue the cows and cowherd boys from a forest fire by swallowing the fire, demonstrating their divine power and protection.

beats.title: The Rainy Season and Autumn in Vrindavan
beats.summary: This chapter describes the arrival of the monsoon season in Vrindavan, followed by autumn. It uses vivid natural imagery to draw parallels between the seasons and spiritual truths, such as the purification of the heart through devotion to Krishna. The chapter concludes with Krishna and Balarama enjoying the rainy season and the subsequent autumn.

beats.title: The Flute Song of Krishna
beats.summary: The Gopis describe the enchanting beauty of Krishna and Balarama in Vrindavan during autumn, focusing on the effect of Krishna's flute music on all beings—gopis, deer, cows, birds, rivers, trees, and even the mountain Govardhana. The chapter highlights the divine love and absorption of the gopis in Krishna's pastimes.

beats.title: The Gopīs' Vow and Kṛṣṇa's Playful Lesson
beats.summary: The gopīs perform a vow to Kātyāyanī to obtain Kṛṣṇa as their husband. Kṛṣṇa playfully steals their clothes while they bathe, teaching them humility and devotion. He then blesses them, fulfilling their desires. Later, Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma go grazing cows, and Kṛṣṇa praises the trees for their selfless service.

beats.title: The Brahmins' Wives and the Offering of Food
beats.summary: Krishna sends the cowherd boys to ask the brahmins performing a sacrifice for food, but they refuse. He then sends them to the brahmins' wives, who eagerly bring food. The wives express their devotion and desire to abandon their families for Krishna, but he instructs them to return and serve their husbands. The brahmins later repent their rudeness and praise the devotion of the women.

beats.title: Krishna Stops the Indra Yajna and Initiates Govardhana Puja
beats.summary: Krishna, seeing the cowherds preparing a sacrifice to Indra, questions Nanda and the elders. He argues that karma, not Indra, determines outcomes, and proposes a sacrifice to Govardhana Hill, cows, and brahmanas instead. The cowherds agree and perform the new sacrifice, with Krishna assuming a giant form as the hill to receive offerings.

beats.title: Krishna Lifts Govardhana Hill
beats.summary: Indra becomes angry when the cowherds, under Krishna's guidance, stop the Indra-yajna and instead worship Govardhana Hill. He sends devastating rain and hail, but Krishna lifts Govardhana Hill like an umbrella, protecting the cowherds and their cattle for seven days. Indra, amazed, withdraws the storm, and the cowherds return home, celebrating Krishna.

beats.title: The Gopas' Astonishment and Nanda's Revelation
beats.summary: The gopas, astonished by Krishna's miraculous deeds, express their doubts to Nanda about his son's divine nature. Nanda recounts sage Garga's prophecy that Krishna is an incarnation of Narayana, dispelling their confusion and filling them with joy.

beats.title: The Coronation of Govinda
beats.summary: Indra, humbled by Krishna's lifting of Govardhana Hill, approaches Krishna with Surabhi the cow to apologize and offer homage. Krishna forgives Indra, and Surabhi requests Krishna to be their protector. Indra then performs an abhisheka (coronation) of Krishna as Govinda, king of cows and cowherds, with celestial waters. The chapter concludes with the celebration of the event by gods and sages, and the natural world rejoices.

beats.title: Krishna Rescues Nanda and Shows the Gopas the Supreme Abode
beats.summary: Nanda is taken by Varuna's servant for bathing at night, but Krishna rescues him. Varuna worships Krishna and apologizes. Krishna then shows the gopas the transcendental realm of Brahman, where they see the Lord's eternal abode.

beats.title: The Rasa Dance Prelude: Krishna's Pastime with the Gopis
beats.summary: Krishna, inspired by the autumn night, decides to enjoy with the gopis. Hearing his flute, the gopis abandon their household duties and rush to him, despite being restrained by their families. Some gopis unable to leave meditate on Krishna. Krishna tests them by advising them to return home, but they refuse, expressing their unwavering devotion. He then relents and sports with them on the Yamuna's bank. Later, to curb their pride, he suddenly disappears.

beats.title: The Gopīs' Search for Kṛṣṇa
beats.summary: The gopīs, maddened by separation from Kṛṣṇa, search for Him in the Vṛndāvana forest, questioning trees and creepers. They enact pastimes of Kṛṣṇa's childhood, then find footprints of Kṛṣṇa and a single gopī. Following them, they discover the gopī who was taken away, now repentant after Kṛṣṇa disappeared. They console her and return to the Yamunā bank, singing of Kṛṣṇa.

beats.title: The Gopis' Song of Separation
beats.summary: The Gopis sing a passionate hymn of separation and longing for Krishna, praising his divine qualities, recalling his pastimes, and begging him to return. They express their complete surrender and devotion, highlighting the pain of his absence and the joy of his presence.

beats.title: The Gopīs' Reunion with Kṛṣṇa
beats.summary: The gopīs, overwhelmed by separation from Kṛṣṇa, sing and lament. Kṛṣṇa appears among them, and they joyfully welcome him. He takes them to the bank of the Yamunā, where they serve him. The gopīs question Kṛṣṇa about his apparent neglect of them, and he explains the nature of pure devotion, emphasizing that he reciprocates in a way that increases their attachment to him.

beats.title: Krishna Rescues Nanda and Kills Shankhachuda
beats.summary: Krishna and Balarama rescue Nanda from a serpent (a cursed Vidyadhara) and later kill the demon Shankhachuda, retrieving his jewel. The chapter highlights Krishna's divine power and protection of his devotees.

beats.title: The Gopis' Song of Kṛṣṇa's Flute
beats.summary: The Gopis describe Kṛṣṇa's flute playing and his pastimes in Vrindavan, expressing their intense longing and love for him. They narrate how all beings—animals, rivers, trees, and even gods—are captivated by his music.

beats.title: The Killing of Arishta and Kamsa's Plot
beats.summary: Krishna kills the demon Arishta in the form of a bull, then Narada reveals to Kamsa that Krishna and Balarama are the sons of Vasudeva and Devaki, destined to kill him. Kamsa imprisons Vasudeva and Devaki, sends the demon Keshini to kill the boys, and plots their death through wrestlers and an elephant. He orders Akrura to bring Krishna and Balarama to Mathura for a bow sacrifice.

beats.title: Krishna Slays the Demons Keshī and Vyoma
beats.summary: Krishna kills the demon Keshī in horse form, then kills the demon Vyoma who kidnapped cowherd boys. The sage Nārada visits Krishna, praises him, and prophesies his future deeds.

beats.title: Akrūra's Journey to Gokula
beats.summary: Akrūra travels from Mathurā to Gokula, meditating on Kṛṣṇa's glories and his own good fortune. Upon arrival, he sees the footprints of Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, then meets them personally. He prostrates, is embraced by Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, and is honored with hospitality by Nanda and the brothers.

beats.title: Akrura's Arrival and the Gopis' Lament
beats.summary: Akrura arrives in Vrindavan to take Krishna and Balarama to Mathura for the festival. The gopis lament their impending separation from Krishna. Krishna and Balarama depart, and the gopis are left in grief. On the way, Akrura bathes in the Yamuna and has a vision of the cosmic form of Vishnu and the divine serpent Ananta Shesha.

beats.title: Akrūra's Prayer to Kṛṣṇa
beats.summary: Akrūra offers a profound prayer to Kṛṣṇa, acknowledging Him as the supreme cause of all causes, the primordial Puruṣa. He describes how all elements, senses, and deities emanate from Him, yet they cannot comprehend His true nature. Akrūra praises various avatāras of the Lord, including Matsya, Hayagrīva, Kūrma, Varāha, Narasiṃha, Vāmana, Paraśurāma, Rāma, Vāsudeva, Saṅkarṣaṇa, Pradyumna, Aniruddha, Buddha, and Kalki. He laments his own ignorance and bondage to material existence, seeking refuge at Kṛṣṇa's lotus feet.

beats.title: Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma Enter Mathurā
beats.summary: Akrūra sees the divine vision of Kṛṣṇa in the Yamunā, then returns to Mathurā with Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma. They enter the city, are welcomed by the citizens, and Kṛṣṇa kills the washerman, blesses the weaver, and grants boons to the garland-maker Sudāmā.

beats.title: Krishna and Balarama in Mathura
beats.summary: Krishna and Balarama enter Mathura, encounter the hunchbacked woman Trivakra who offers them sandalwood paste, and Krishna straightens her body. They then break the great bow in the arena, kill Kamsa's guards, and spend the night in the city. Kamsa, terrified by omens, arranges a wrestling match the next day. The chapter ends with the wrestlers and Nanda's party arriving.

beats.title: Krishna and Balarama Enter the Arena
beats.summary: Krishna and Balarama, having heard the wrestling arena's drum, go to see it. At the entrance, Krishna confronts and kills the elephant Kuvalayapida, then enters the arena with Balarama. The people praise them, recalling their past deeds. Canura challenges them to wrestle, and Krishna agrees.

beats.title: Krishna and Balarama Kill Kamsa's Wrestlers and Kamsa
beats.summary: Krishna and Balarama defeat the wrestlers Canura, Mustika, Kuta, Sala, and Tosala in the Mathura arena, then Krishna kills Kamsa. The women of Kamsa's family lament, and Krishna and Balarama release their parents Devaki and Vasudeva from bondage.

beats.title: Krishna and Balarama's Return to Mathura and Education
beats.summary: Krishna and Balarama return to Mathura, reunite with their parents Vasudeva and Devaki, and console them. They install Ugrasena as king, welcome back the Yadava clans who had fled due to Kamsa, and send Nanda and the gopas back to Vrindavan. They then undergo the sacred thread ceremony with Garga as teacher and study under Sandipani in Avanti, learning all scriptures and arts in 64 days. To fulfill the guru's request for dakshina, they retrieve the guru's son from the ocean, killing the demon Panchajana, and bring the boy back from Yama's city.

beats.title: Uddhava's Visit to Vrindavan
beats.summary: Uddhava is sent by Krishna to Vrindavan to console Nanda, Yashoda, and the gopis who are suffering from separation. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message, describing his divine nature and assuring them of his return. The chapter ends with the gopis' morning activities and their curiosity about Uddhava's arrival.

beats.title: Uddhava's Visit to Vrindavan
beats.summary: Uddhava visits Vrindavan as Krishna's messenger. The gopis express their intense separation from Krishna, recounting their love and pain. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message of spiritual union through constant remembrance. The gopis respond with both joy and longing. Uddhava stays for some months, consoling them with Krishna's pastimes, then returns to Mathura.

beats.title: Krishna's Visit to Akrura and the Mission to Hastinapura
beats.summary: Krishna visits the courtesan's house, fulfills her desire, then goes to Akrura's house. Akrura praises Krishna and is sent to Hastinapura to check on the Pandavas.

beats.title: Akrūra's Mission to Hastinapura
beats.summary: Akrūra visits Hastinapura, meets with Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Bhīṣma, Vidura, Kuntī, and others. Kuntī laments her sons' exile and prays to Kṛṣṇa. Akrūra advises Dhṛtarāṣṭra to treat the Pāṇḍavas equally, but Dhṛtarāṣṭra admits his attachment to his sons. Akrūra returns to Dvārakā and reports to Balarāma and Kṛṣṇa.

beats.title: Jarāsandha's Attacks and the Building of Dvārakā
beats.summary: Jarāsandha, king of Magadha, attacks Mathurā with a vast army to avenge his sons-in-law Kaṃsa's death. Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, recognizing the divine purpose to relieve the earth's burden, engage in battle. Kṛṣṇa destroys the army but spares Jarāsandha, who is captured by Balarāma and then released. Jarāsandha attacks seventeen more times, each time defeated. On the eighteenth, the Yavana king Kālayavana arrives, prompting Kṛṣṇa to build a fortress city in the sea (Dvārakā) to protect the Yadus. The chapter ends with Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma leaving Mathurā to confront Kālayavana.

beats.title: Mucukunda's Liberation
beats.summary: Mucukunda, a king from the Ikshvaku dynasty, had helped the gods by guarding their realm. After being granted a boon, he chose to sleep in a cave. Later, the Yavana king pursued Krishna into the cave, where Mucukunda was sleeping. The Yavana kicked Mucukunda, who then burned him with his angry glance. Krishna then revealed himself to Mucukunda, who offered prayers and sought liberation. Krishna granted him unwavering devotion and a future birth as a brahmana.

beats.title: Krishna's Marriage to Rukmini
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the events leading to Krishna's marriage with Rukmini. After the demon Mura's defeat, Krishna returns to Dvaraka, defeats Jarāsandha's army, and later marries Rukmini, the princess of Vidarbha, by abducting her in the Rākṣasa style after defeating her brother Rukmi and other kings. The chapter includes Rukmini's message to Krishna expressing her love and her plan for him to abduct her.

beats.title: The Abduction of Rukmini
beats.summary: Krishna, having received Rukmini's message, decides to abduct her before her marriage to Shishupala. He travels to Kundina with his charioteer Daruka. Meanwhile, King Bhishmaka prepares for the wedding, and many enemy kings assemble. Rukmini anxiously awaits Krishna. A brahmana messenger confirms Krishna's arrival. Rukmini goes to the temple of Ambika (Parvati) to pray, then exits. As she boards her chariot, Krishna abducts her in the presence of all the kings, who are stunned. Balarama and the Yadava army support Krishna.

beats.title: The Abduction of Rukmini
beats.summary: Krishna abducts Rukmini from her svayamvara, defeats the assembled kings including Shishupala and Rukmi, spares Rukmi's life at Rukmini's request, and marries her in Dvaraka with great celebration.

beats.title: The Return of Pradyumna
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Pradyumna, the son of Krishna and Rukmini, who is an incarnation of Kamadeva. As an infant, he is stolen by the demon Shambara and thrown into the ocean, where a fish swallows him. The fish is caught and brought to Shambara's kitchen, where the child is discovered by Mayavati (Rati in disguise). Raised by her, Pradyumna grows up, learns his true identity from Rati, and defeats Shambara in battle, cutting off his head. He then returns to Dvaraka, where he is joyfully reunited with his family.

beats.title: The Syamantaka Jewel
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of the Syamantaka jewel. Satrajit, a devotee of the Sun god, receives the jewel from Surya. His brother Prasena is killed by a lion while wearing it, and the lion is killed by Jambavan. Krishna is falsely accused of taking the jewel. To clear his name, Krishna tracks the jewel to Jambavan's cave, fights him for 28 days, and ultimately reveals his divine identity. Jambavan offers his daughter Jambavati and the jewel to Krishna. Krishna returns the jewel to Satrajit, who, repentant, gives his daughter Satyabhama and the jewel to Krishna. Krishna accepts Satyabhama but declines the jewel.

beats.title: The Syamantaka Jewel: Murder and Redemption
beats.summary: Krishna and Balarama visit Hastinapura after the Kuru fire incident, then return to Dvaraka. Satrajit is murdered by Shatadhanva for the Syamantaka jewel. Shatadhanva flees, pursued by Krishna and Balarama. Krishna kills Shatadhanva but the jewel is not found. Balarama goes to Mithila, Krishna returns to Dvaraka. Later, Krishna retrieves the jewel from Akrura, clearing his name.

beats.title: Krishna's Marriages and the Pandava Connection
beats.summary: Krishna visits the Pandavas in Indraprastha, marries Kalindi (daughter of Surya), and later marries Satya (daughter of Nagnajit) after subduing seven bulls. He also marries Bhadra, Lakshmana, and many other wives rescued from the demon Bhauma.

beats.title: The Slaying of Narakasura and the Rescue of the Princesses
beats.summary: Krishna, accompanied by his wife Satyabhama, travels to the city of Pragjyotisha to kill the demon Narakasura. After defeating Naraka and his forces, Krishna recovers the earrings of Aditi, frees 16,000 captive princesses, and returns to Dvaraka. He then marries the princesses and later uproots the Parijata tree from Indra's heaven for Satyabhama.

beats.title: The Playful Test of Rukmini's Devotion
beats.summary: Krishna playfully tests Rukmini's devotion by pretending to be unworthy of her, causing her distress. She responds with a heartfelt discourse on her unwavering love and surrender, leading to Krishna's reassurance and their loving reconciliation.

beats.title: The Sons of Krishna and the Marriage of Aniruddha
beats.summary: This chapter lists the sons of Krishna by his chief queens and describes the marriage of Aniruddha to Rocana, the dice game between Balarama and Rukmi, and the subsequent killing of Rukmi by Balarama.

beats.title: The Abduction of Aniruddha and the Battle with Bana
beats.summary: King Parikshit asks Shuka about the marriage of Aniruddha to Usha, daughter of Bana, and the ensuing battle between Krishna and Shiva. Shuka narrates: Bana, a devotee of Shiva, had a thousand arms and became arrogant. Shiva prophesied his flag would fall when he fought an equal. Bana's daughter Usha dreamt of Aniruddha and fell in love. Her friend Citralekha, a yogini, identified Aniruddha and brought him to Usha. They lived together secretly. Bana discovered them and captured Aniruddha with serpent ropes. Usha lamented.

beats.title: The Rescue of Aniruddha and the Battle with Bana
beats.summary: Krishna and Balarama lead the Vrishnis to rescue Aniruddha from Bana's fortress. A fierce battle ensues between Krishna and Shiva, and between their respective armies. Krishna defeats Shiva's forces, cuts off Bana's arms except four, and spares him due to Prahlada's boon. Bana surrenders, returns Aniruddha and Usha, and the Vrishnis return home.

beats.title: The Deliverance of King Nṛga
beats.summary: King Nṛga, a former king of the Ikṣvāku dynasty, recounts his fall from grace due to an inadvertent misdeed involving a Brahmin's cow. After giving away a cow that had strayed into his herd, two Brahmins claimed it, leading to a dispute. Unable to resolve it, Nṛga died and was turned into a lizard (kṛkalāsa) in a dry well. Lord Kṛṣṇa rescues him, restoring his divine form, and Nṛga praises Kṛṣṇa before ascending to heaven. Kṛṣṇa then teaches the Yadus about the grave sin of misappropriating Brahmin property.

beats.title: Balarama's Visit to Vraja and the Yamuna's Submission
beats.summary: Balarama visits Nanda's Gokula, is welcomed by the gopas and gopis who inquire about Krishna. He consoles them with Krishna's messages, stays for two months, and enjoys pastimes with the gopis. He summons the Yamuna river, which initially disobeys, but then submits; Balarama drags her with his plow, then releases her. He sports in the water and is adorned by the river goddess.

beats.title: The Killing of Paundraka and the Burning of Varanasi
beats.summary: Paundraka, a foolish king, claims to be Vasudeva and sends a messenger to Krishna demanding he surrender. Krishna kills Paundraka and his ally the king of Kashi. The king's son Sudakshina performs a ritual to invoke a fiery demon to avenge his father, but Krishna's Sudarshana chakra destroys the demon and burns Varanasi.

beats.title: The Slaying of Dvivida
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about Balarāma's extraordinary deeds. Śuka narrates how the demon Dvivida, a friend of Naraka and a minister of Sugrīva, wreaks havoc across the land to avenge his friend's death. He eventually confronts Balarāma on Raivataka mountain, where Balarāma is drinking and singing with women. Dvivida mocks and harasses them, leading to a fierce battle. Balarāma kills Dvivida with his mace, and the gods praise him.

beats.title: The Abduction of Lakshmana and Balarama's Wrath
beats.summary: Samba, son of Krishna and Jambavati, abducts Lakshmana, daughter of Duryodhana, from her svayamvara. The Kauravas capture him. Balarama goes to Hastinapura to negotiate but is insulted. He then uproots the city with his plow and threatens to throw it into the Ganges. The Kauravas surrender and offer gifts. Balarama returns to Dvaraka.

beats.title: Narada's Visit to Krishna's Palaces
beats.summary: Narada visits Dvaraka to see how Krishna simultaneously lives with his 16,108 wives. He witnesses Krishna in each palace, engaged in different activities, demonstrating his yoga-maya. Narada praises Krishna and departs, and the chapter concludes with the glory of hearing this pastime.

beats.title: Krishna's Daily Routine and the Imprisoned Kings
beats.summary: Krishna's daily routine in Dwaraka, including his morning activities, worship, and court session. A messenger from imprisoned kings arrives seeking help against Jarāsandha. Nārada appears and informs Krishna about Yudhiṣṭhira's plan to perform the Rājasūya sacrifice. Krishna consults Uddhava.

beats.title: Uddhava's Counsel and Krishna's Journey to Indraprastha
beats.summary: Uddhava advises Krishna to kill Jarāsandha to enable the Rājasūya sacrifice, and Krishna agrees. The Yādava army travels to Indraprastha, where Yudhiṣṭhira and the Pāṇḍavas welcome them. Krishna stays for several months, enjoying pastimes with Arjuna.

beats.title: The Killing of Jarāsandha
beats.summary: Yudhiṣṭhira expresses his desire to perform the Rājasūya sacrifice. Kṛṣṇa advises him to first conquer all kings. Yudhiṣṭhira sends his brothers to subdue the directions. They bring tribute, but Jarāsandha remains unsubdued. Kṛṣṇa, Bhīma, and Arjuna, disguised as brāhmaṇas, go to Girivraja. They challenge Jarāsandha to a duel. Bhīma fights Jarāsandha and, with Kṛṣṇa's hint, splits his body in two. Kṛṣṇa installs Jarāsandha's son Sahadeva as king and releases the imprisoned kings.

beats.title: Release of the Imprisoned Kings
beats.summary: Krishna releases 20,800 kings imprisoned by Jarasandha, who praise Krishna and renounce worldly desires. Krishna instructs them on devotion and righteous rule, then honors them and sends them back. He then has Bhima kill Jarasandha and returns to Indraprastha with Arjuna and Sahadeva, where Yudhishthira rejoices.

beats.title: The Rajasuya Sacrifice and the Death of Shishupala
beats.summary: Yudhishthira performs the Rajasuya sacrifice. During the ceremony, Sahadeva proposes that Krishna be given the highest honor. Shishupala objects and insults Krishna, leading to Krishna killing Shishupala with his discus. The sacrifice concludes successfully.

beats.title: The Rājasūya Sacrifice and Duryodhana's Humiliation
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Rājasūya sacrifice of King Yudhiṣṭhira, the distribution of duties among his brothers and relatives, the grand celebration including music, dance, and bathing in the Gaṅgā, the departure of guests, and Duryodhana's envy and humiliation when he mistakes water for solid ground in the magical assembly hall, leading to his anger and departure.

beats.title: The Slaying of Śālva
beats.summary: King Śālva, a friend of Śiśupāla, vows to destroy the Yadus after being defeated at Rukmiṇī's wedding. He propitiates Lord Śiva and obtains a flying city called Saubha, made by Maya Dānava. He attacks Dvārakā, causing havoc. Pradyumna leads the Yādava defense, countering Śālva's illusions and fighting fiercely. Pradyumna is struck down by Śālva's minister Dyumān but is rescued by his charioteer, who explains he acted out of duty to protect his master.

beats.title: The Slaying of Shalva
beats.summary: Krishna battles the Saubha king Shalva, who uses illusion to deceive Krishna into thinking his father Vasudeva has been captured and killed. Krishna overcomes the illusion, destroys the Saubha flying city, and kills Shalva. The chapter also mentions the aftermath of the Rajasuya sacrifice and Shishupala's death.

beats.title: The Death of Dantavakra and Balarama's Pilgrimage
beats.summary: Krishna kills Dantavakra and his brother Viduratha, then returns to Dvaraka. Meanwhile, Balarama, to avoid taking sides in the Kuru-Pandava war, goes on a pilgrimage. At Naimisha Forest, he kills the disrespectful suta Romaharshana (Lomaharshana) with a blade of grass, incurring brahmana-hatya. The sages prescribe a penance: kill the demon Balvala and then perform a twelve-month pilgrimage.

beats.title: Balarama's Pilgrimage and Intervention at Kurukshetra
beats.summary: Balarama travels to various holy places, kills the demon Balvala, witnesses the Kurukshetra war, attempts to stop the duel between Bhima and Duryodhana, returns to Dvaraka, and later performs a sacrifice at Naimisha forest, teaching pure knowledge to the sages.

beats.title: The Brāhmaṇa and the Lord
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about the glories of Kṛṣṇa. Śuka narrates the story of a poor brāhmaṇa, a former classmate of Kṛṣṇa, who is urged by his wife to seek help from Kṛṣṇa. The brāhmaṇa goes to Dvārakā, is warmly received by Kṛṣṇa, and they reminisce about their time at the guru's hermitage. Kṛṣṇa emphasizes the importance of guru-sevā and the brāhmaṇa's devotion.

beats.title: The Brahmana Sudama's Visit to Krishna
beats.summary: Krishna receives the poor Brahmana Sudama, who brings a handful of flattened rice as a gift. Krishna eats it and grants him immense wealth. Sudama returns to find his hut transformed into a palace, realizes Krishna's grace, and attains liberation.

beats.title: The Pilgrimage at Samanta-pancaka
beats.summary: The chapter describes a great solar eclipse at Dvaraka, prompting many kings and relatives to gather at Samanta-pancaka for a pilgrimage. The Vrishnis, including Krishna and Balarama, attend. There are emotional reunions, especially between the gopis and Krishna, who teaches them about devotion and the nature of the self.

beats.title: The Queens' Narratives of Their Marriages to Krishna
beats.summary: The queens of Krishna, including Rukmini, Satyabhama, Jambavati, Kalindi, Mitravinda, Satya, Bhadra, and Lakshmana, recount to Draupadi how they each came to marry Krishna. Their stories highlight Krishna's divine pastimes, his victory over various kings and demons, and the queens' deep devotion. The chapter concludes with the queens expressing their desire for the dust of Krishna's lotus feet rather than any worldly or heavenly position.

beats.title: Vasudeva's Sacrifice and the Sages' Visit
beats.summary: The chapter describes the arrival of many sages at the sacrificial arena of Vasudeva, their glorification of Krishna, and the performance of the sacrifice. It concludes with the departure of guests and Nanda's stay with the Yadus.

beats.title: The Rescue of Devaki's Sons
beats.summary: Vasudeva and Devaki recognize Krishna and Balarama as the Supreme Lord and His first expansion. Devaki, grieving her six sons killed by Kamsa, asks to see them. Krishna and Balarama descend to Sutala, retrieve the six sons from Bali Maharaja, and bring them back to Devaki. The sons, previously sages cursed to be demons, are purified and return to heaven.

beats.title: Arjuna's Marriage to Subhadra and Krishna's Visit to Mithila
beats.summary: This chapter of the Bhagavata Purana (10.86) narrates two episodes: first, Arjuna's marriage to Subhadra, the sister of Krishna and Balarama, and second, Krishna's visit to Mithila where he stays with both King Bahulashva (Janaka) and the brahmana Shrutadeva, teaching the importance of honoring brahmanas as his own forms.

beats.title: The Hymns of the Vedas: The Dialogue of Nārada and Nārāyaṇa
beats.summary: In this chapter, King Parīkṣit asks Śuka how the Vedas (śrutis) can describe the indescribable, attributeless Brahman. Śuka responds by narrating a dialogue between Nārada and Nārāyaṇa Ṛṣi. Nārāyaṇa recounts a past event in Janaloka where the mind-born sons of Brahmā (Sanandana and others) heard the Vedas themselves praising the Supreme Lord. The Vedas' hymns form the core of the chapter, expounding the nature of Brahman, the role of māyā, and the path of devotion. The chapter concludes with Nārada receiving this teaching and sharing it with Vyāsa.

beats.title: The Story of Vṛkāsura
beats.summary: This chapter discusses the nature of worshiping different deities, contrasting the worship of Lord Hari (Vishnu) with that of other gods like Shiva and Brahma. It explains that worshiping the nirguna (transcendental) Lord Hari leads to liberation, while worshiping the saguna (qualified) deities may grant material boons but can lead to pride and downfall. The story of Vṛkāsura illustrates this: the demon, after receiving a boon from Shiva that allowed him to kill anyone by placing his hand on their head, tried to test it on Shiva himself. Shiva fled to Vishnu, who appeared as a brahmacārī and tricked Vṛkāsura into placing his hand on his own head, thus killing him. The chapter emphasizes that even great gods like Shiva can be put in danger by their own boons, but Lord Vishnu is always the ultimate protector.

beats.title: The Greatness of Vishnu and the Rescue of the Brahmana's Sons
beats.summary: The chapter narrates two main episodes: first, the sage Bhrigu tests the three chief deities (Brahma, Shiva, Vishnu) to determine who is greatest, concluding that Vishnu is supreme due to his humility and forgiveness. Second, Arjuna, challenged by a Brahmana whose children die at birth, vows to protect them but fails initially; Krishna then takes him to the cosmic realm of Ananta-Shesha, where they retrieve the children and restore them, demonstrating Krishna's supremacy and the power of surrender.

beats.title: Krishna's Pastimes in Dvaraka and the Queens' Lament
beats.summary: This chapter describes Krishna's divine pastimes in Dvaraka with his 16,000 queens, the queens' expressions of separation through allegorical addresses to birds and natural elements, the genealogy of Krishna's sons and the Yadu dynasty, and concludes with the glories of hearing and chanting Krishna's pastimes.

beats.title: The Curse of the Sages and the End of the Yadava Dynasty
beats.summary: Krishna, having killed the demons and relieved the earth's burden, decides to end his own Yadava dynasty through a curse by sages. The Yadava boys dress Samba as a pregnant woman and ask the sages what she will give birth to. The sages curse that she will give birth to an iron club that will destroy the dynasty. The club is ground into powder and thrown into the sea, but a fish swallows a piece, which later becomes an arrowhead, and the powder washes ashore and grows into reeds that will be used in the future fratricidal war.

beats.title: The Nine Yogendras Instruct King Nimi on Bhāgavata-dharma
beats.summary: Narada recounts to Vasudeva the conversation between King Nimi and the nine Yogendras (sons of Rishabha) about the nature of Bhāgavata-dharma, the path of devotion to Lord Krishna. The nine sages—Kavi, Havir, Antariksha, Prabuddha, Pippalayana, Avirhotra, Drumila, Camasa, and Karabhajana—explain the characteristics of a devotee, the stages of bhakti, and the means to attain liberation.

beats.title: The Dialogue of King Nimi and the Nine Yogendras
beats.summary: This chapter of the Bhagavata Purana (11.3) features a dialogue between King Nimi and the nine Yogendras (sages). The king asks about the nature of Maya, the means to transcend it, and the path of karma-yoga. The sages explain the cosmic cycle of creation and dissolution, the nature of the self, the importance of surrendering to a guru, the practice of Bhakti, and the process of deity worship. The chapter emphasizes that through devotion to Narayana and the guidance of a realized teacher, one can cross the difficult Maya.

beats.title: The Avatāras of Hari
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śrī Śuka about the avatāras of Hari. Śrī Drumila (or Śuka) responds by describing the infinite glories of the Lord, the cosmic creation, and the primary avatāras: Brahmā (creator), Viṣṇu (preserver), and Rudra (destroyer). He then narrates the story of Nara-Nārāyaṇa Ṛṣi, who was tested by Indra with celestial beauties, and lists the major avatāras including Haṃsa, Dattātreya, Kumāra, Ṛṣabha, Hayagrīva, Matsya, Kūrma, Varāha, Nṛsiṃha, Vāmana, Paraśurāma, Rāma, and Kṛṣṇa.

beats.title: The Glories of the Four Yugas and the Path of Devotion
beats.summary: This chapter describes the four yugas (Krita, Treta, Dvapara, Kali) and the corresponding forms and modes of worship of the Supreme Lord Hari. It emphasizes that in Kali Yuga, the most effective practice is sankirtana (congregational chanting of the Lord's names). The chapter also recounts the story of King Nimi and the nine Yogendras, highlighting the glory of devotional service and the liberation of Vasudeva and Devaki.

beats.title: The Gods' Visit and Krishna's Departure Plan
beats.summary: The gods and sages visit Krishna in Dvārakā, praising him. Brahmā reminds Krishna of his mission to relieve the earth's burden and requests his return. Krishna agrees, citing the Yādava clan's arrogance and the brāhmaṇa's curse. He instructs the Yādavas to go to Prabhāsa for purification. Uddhava expresses his devotion and desire to accompany Krishna.

beats.title: The Avadhuta's Teaching to Yadu
beats.summary: Lord Krishna instructs Uddhava on renunciation and detachment, using the allegory of a pigeon couple destroyed by excessive attachment. He teaches that one should learn from 24 gurus, including natural elements and animals, to attain liberation.

beats.title: The Story of Pingala
beats.summary: A brahmana instructs a king on the path of detachment and renunciation, using the story of the prostitute Pingala who, after a night of disappointment, realizes the futility of worldly desires and turns to the Supreme Self. The chapter emphasizes the importance of controlling the senses, especially the tongue, and the need for a detached, contented life.

beats.title: The Avadhūta's Teachings to King Yadu
beats.summary: The chapter presents the teachings of an avadhūta brāhmaṇa to King Yadu, illustrating the path to liberation through detachment, solitude, and self-realization. Using parables such as the kurara bird and the maiden with bangles, the brāhmaṇa explains the futility of possessions and relationships, and emphasizes the importance of controlling the mind and senses. He concludes by describing the supreme state of the self, beyond the three guṇas, and the necessity of multiple gurus for complete knowledge.

beats.title: The Path of Self-Realization
beats.summary: In Bhagavata Purana 11.10, Lord Krishna instructs Uddhava on the path to liberation through detachment, self-inquiry, and surrender to the Supreme. He explains the futility of worldly pleasures, the cycle of karma, and the importance of a guru. Uddhava then asks how a liberated being acts while still embodied.

beats.title: The Path of Bhakti and the Nature of Bondage and Liberation
beats.summary: In this chapter, Lord Krishna (Śrī Bhagavān) explains to Uddhava the nature of bondage and liberation, the characteristics of a true devotee, and the various practices of bhakti-yoga. He emphasizes that bondage and liberation are due to māyā and not inherent in the self. The chapter describes the qualities of a sage, the means to attain unwavering devotion, and the importance of satsaṅga (association with the wise).

beats.title: The Power of Association with Devotees
beats.summary: Lord Krishna explains to Uddhava that the supreme goal of life is to attain Him through exclusive devotion and association with His devotees (sat-sanga). He emphasizes that even beings of low birth, such as demons, animals, and women, have attained Him through pure love, while those relying solely on yoga, knowledge, or rituals may fail. He then describes the nature of the jiva and the tree of samsara, and advises Uddhava to cut it down with the axe of knowledge and devotion.

beats.title: The Haṃsa-gītā: Teaching on Guṇas and Yoga
beats.summary: Lord Krishna explains the nature of the three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) and how to transcend them through yoga and devotion. He recounts his teaching to the Sanaka sages in the form of Haṃsa, emphasizing the oneness of the self and the illusory nature of duality.

beats.title: The Glory of Bhakti-Yoga
beats.summary: Krishna explains the superiority of bhakti-yoga over other paths, describes the process of meditation on his form, and emphasizes that pure devotion alone can lead to liberation.

beats.title: The Yoga of Siddhis
beats.summary: Lord Krishna describes the eighteen siddhis (perfections) attainable through yoga, focusing on the eight primary siddhis (aṇimā, mahimā, laghimā, prāpti, prākāmya, īśitā, vaśitā, and kāmāvasāyitā) and ten secondary ones. He explains the specific dhāraṇā (concentration) practices for each, emphasizing that these powers are obstacles for the true yogi seeking union with Him. The chapter concludes by stating that all siddhis come from Krishna alone.

beats.title: The Divine Manifestations (Vibhuti Yoga)
beats.summary: Lord Krishna, as the supreme being, reveals his divine manifestations (vibhutis) to Uddhava, explaining that he is the essence and source of all beings, elements, and powers. He concludes by instructing Uddhava to control speech, mind, and senses through devotion, leading to liberation.

beats.title: The Eternal Duties of Varṇāśrama Dharma
beats.summary: Uddhava asks Krishna to explain the eternal duties (dharma) characterized by devotion to Him, as previously taught in the form of a swan to Brahma. Krishna responds by describing the original nature of humanity in Satya-yuga (the swan-like state), the emergence of the four varṇas and āśramas from His body, the natural qualities of each varṇa, and the duties of a brahmacārī, gṛhastha, and king, emphasizing detachment and devotion.

beats.title: The Duties of the Vānaprastha and Sannyāsī
beats.summary: Lord Krishna describes the duties and lifestyle of the vānaprastha (forest-dweller) and sannyāsa (renounced) orders, emphasizing detachment, self-control, and devotion to Him as the ultimate goal. He outlines practices such as eating forest foods, enduring hardships, and performing sacrifices without animals, and explains the importance of inner renunciation over external forms. The chapter culminates in the teaching that following one's dharma with bhakti leads to liberation.

beats.title: The Definition of True Knowledge and Devotion
beats.summary: In this chapter, Lord Krishna defines the nature of true knowledge, devotion, and the path to liberation. He explains that jñāna (knowledge) is seeing the one reality behind all beings, and vijñāna (realization) is perceiving the threefold transformations of matter. He then describes the various components of bhakti-yoga, including yama and niyama, and answers Uddhava's questions on the definitions of key spiritual terms such as dharma, jñāna, vairāgya, aiśvarya, and others. The chapter emphasizes that devotion to Krishna is the ultimate goal and that all other practices are means to that end.

beats.title: The Three Paths of Yoga and the Supremacy of Bhakti
beats.summary: Lord Krishna explains the three paths of yoga (jñāna, karma, bhakti) suited to different types of aspirants, emphasizing that bhakti-yoga is the highest and most direct means to attain liberation. He describes the process of detachment, control of mind, and the ultimate goal of exclusive devotion, which surpasses even liberation.

beats.title: The Nature of Dharma and the Vedas
beats.summary: Lord Krishna explains the nature of dharma and adharma, the role of the Vedas, the process of purification, and the dangers of sense attachment. He emphasizes that true dharma leads to liberation, while mere ritualistic observance without devotion is futile.

beats.title: The Enumeration of Tattvas
beats.summary: Uddhava asks Krishna about the number of tattvas (elements) as enumerated by various sages. Krishna explains that the differences arise due to the interplay of his maya and the mutual pervasion of categories. He describes the evolution from prakriti through the gunas, the senses, and the elements, and discusses the nature of the self, rebirth, and the means to liberation through detachment and self-knowledge.

beats.title: The Song of the Avanti Brahmin
beats.summary: Krishna narrates to Uddhava the story of a greedy brahmin from Avanti who lost his wealth and family, became disillusioned, and took sannyasa. Despite being abused by wicked people, he remained steadfast and sang a song of wisdom, teaching that the mind is the sole cause of bondage and liberation, and that one should control the mind through devotion to Krishna.

beats.title: Sāṅkhya: The Path of Analytical Knowledge
beats.summary: Lord Krishna explains the Sāṅkhya philosophy of creation and dissolution, describing how the one undivided reality manifests as Prakṛti and Puruṣa, evolves into the material world, and ultimately dissolves back into the absolute. He emphasizes that understanding this process destroys doubt and illusion.

beats.title: The Modes of Nature and Bhakti-Yoga
beats.summary: Lord Krishna explains to Uddhava the three modes of material nature (sattva, rajas, tamas) and their manifestations in human behavior, knowledge, actions, and spiritual paths. He emphasizes transcending the modes through bhakti-yoga to attain liberation.

beats.title: The Story of King Pururavas and Urvashi
beats.summary: Lord Krishna teaches Uddhava about detachment from sense objects, especially women, using the story of King Pururavas (Aila) who became infatuated with the celestial nymph Urvashi. After realizing his folly, Pururavas renounced attachment and attained self-realization. Krishna emphasizes the importance of associating with saintly persons who help cut material attachments and lead to devotion.

beats.title: The Path of Kriyā-Yoga: Ritual Worship of the Lord
beats.summary: Lord Krishna explains the detailed procedure of kriyā-yoga (ritualistic worship) to Uddhava, covering the three types of sacrifice (Vedic, Tantric, mixed), the eight types of idols, the steps of worship including purification, meditation, offerings, fire sacrifice, and the benefits of such worship. He emphasizes that worship with devotion, even with simple offerings, is superior to elaborate worship without devotion.

beats.title: The Yoga of Discrimination and Devotion
beats.summary: In this chapter, Lord Krishna teaches Uddhava about the nature of the self, the illusion of duality, and the path to liberation through discrimination and devotion. He explains that the world is a manifestation of the one Self, and that one should neither praise nor blame others' actions. The chapter emphasizes the importance of steady practice of yoga, detachment, and surrender to Krishna to overcome obstacles.

beats.title: The Yoga of Devotion
beats.summary: Uddhava asks Krishna about the difficulty of yoga practice for the unsteady-minded. Krishna responds by teaching the path of bhakti-yoga, emphasizing devotion, equality of vision, and surrender. He instructs Uddhava to go to Badarikashrama and practice. Uddhava, overwhelmed with love, departs after circumambulating Krishna.

beats.title: The Destruction of the Yadu Dynasty and Krishna's Departure
beats.summary: This chapter describes the destruction of the Yadu dynasty due to a curse, the death of Balarama and Krishna, and the aftermath. Krishna arranges a pilgrimage to Prabhasa, where the Yadavas get drunk and fight among themselves, killing each other. Balarama leaves his body through yoga, and Krishna is accidentally shot by a hunter named Jara. Krishna then instructs his charioteer Daruka to inform the people of Dvaraka and departs.

beats.title: The Departure of Krishna
beats.summary: This chapter describes the departure of Lord Krishna from the mortal world, his yogic absorption into his own abode, the reactions of various beings, the subsequent grief of the Vrishnis, the funeral rites, the submergence of Dvārakā, and the establishment of Vajra as king. It concludes with the benefits of reciting Krishna's deeds.

beats.title: Dynasties of Kali Yuga
beats.summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (12th Canto) lists the dynasties that will rule after the Mahabharata war, starting with Pradyota and ending with the Mleccha kings, describing the decline of dharma in Kali Yuga.

beats.title: The Degradation of Kali Yuga and the Advent of Kalki
beats.summary: This chapter describes the degradation of dharma in the Kali Yuga, the eventual appearance of Kalki avatar to restore righteousness, and the cyclical nature of the four yugas. It also lists the dynasties of kings and the duration of Kali Yuga.

beats.title: The Glories of Kali Yuga and the Power of Krishna's Names
beats.summary: This chapter describes the characteristics of the four yugas (Krita, Treta, Dvapara, and Kali) and the decline of dharma in Kali Yuga. It emphasizes that the only remedy for the sins of Kali is constant chanting of the names of Krishna, which purifies the heart and leads to liberation. The chapter also lists many past kings and demons who were attached to the earth and ultimately perished, highlighting the futility of material conquest.

beats.title: The Four Types of Dissolution
beats.summary: This chapter describes the four types of cosmic dissolution (pralaya): nitya (constant), naimittika (occasional), prākṛtika (elemental), and ātyantika (ultimate). It details the process of elemental dissolution during prākṛtika pralaya, where the five gross elements merge into their subtle causes, and ultimately into the unmanifest prakṛti. The chapter emphasizes the non-dual nature of Brahman, the illusory nature of multiplicity, and the means to attain liberation through devotion to the Lord's pastimes.

beats.title: Instruction on the Eternal Self
beats.summary: Śrī Śuka instructs King Parīkṣit on the nature of the self, distinguishing it from the body and mind. He explains that the ātman is eternal, unborn, and unchanging, while the body and mind are products of māyā. Through the analogy of a lamp and the example of a dream, he teaches that the self is unaffected by death. The chapter culminates in the realization of one's identity as Brahman, the supreme reality, which protects one from death and the serpent Takṣaka.

beats.title: The Liberation of Parīkṣit and the Transmission of the Vedas
beats.summary: King Parīkṣit, after hearing the Bhāgavata Purāṇa from Śuka, attains liberation. His body is burned by Takṣaka's venom. His son Janamejaya performs a snake sacrifice to avenge him, but is stopped by Bṛhaspati. The chapter then traces the transmission of the Vedas from Brahmā through various sages, including Vyāsa's division of the Vedas and the subsequent branches of the Ṛg, Yajur, Sāma, and Atharva Vedas. Yājñavalkya receives new Yajus from the Sun god after regurgitating the old ones, leading to the Taittirīya and Vājasaneyi recensions.

beats.title: The Lineages of Vedic and Purāṇic Teachers and the Characteristics of Purāṇas
beats.summary: This chapter lists the lineages of teachers of the Atharvaveda and the Puranas, defines the ten characteristics of a Purana, and enumerates the eighteen major Puranas.

beats.title: Mārkaṇḍeya's Penance and Vision of Nara-Nārāyaṇa
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya, a great sage, performs severe penance and conquers death. Indra, fearing his power, sends celestial beings to distract him, but Mārkaṇḍeya remains undisturbed. Pleased, Lord Nara-Nārāyaṇa appears before him, and Mārkaṇḍeya offers a hymn of praise.

beats.title: Mārkaṇḍeya's Vision of Viṣṇu's Māyā
beats.summary: Lord Nārāyaṇa appears to sage Mārkaṇḍeya, grants him a boon, and shows him the cosmic illusion (māyā) of Viṣṇu. Mārkaṇḍeya experiences the dissolution of the universe, sees a divine child on a banyan leaf, enters the child's body, and witnesses the entire cosmos within. The vision ends, and Mārkaṇḍeya returns to his hermitage.

beats.title: Markandeya's Vision and Shiva's Blessing
beats.summary: Markandeya Rishi, after experiencing the divine illusion of Narayana, seeks refuge at his feet. Lord Shiva and Parvati visit him, and Shiva grants him boons including unalloyed devotion to Vishnu, eternal fame, and the status of a Purana teacher.

beats.title: Chapter 11
beats.summary: This chapter describes the cosmic form of the Lord (Virāṭ Puruṣa) and the worship of the sun-god as a manifestation of Viṣṇu. It details the parts of the universe as limbs of the Lord, the divine attributes and weapons, the fourfold expansion (Vāsudeva, Saṅkarṣaṇa, Pradyumna, Aniruddha), and the twelve months presided over by various deities, sages, and celestial beings. The chapter concludes with the benefits of remembering these forms.

beats.title: Summary of the Bhagavata Purana
beats.summary: This chapter is the final chapter of the Bhagavata Purana, summarizing the entire text's contents, glorifying the hearing and recitation of the Purana, and emphasizing the supreme importance of devotion to Krishna (Vasudeva). It lists the major topics covered in the Purana, including creation, avatars, dynasties, and the life of Krishna, and concludes with verses on the purifying power of chanting the Lord's names.

beats.title: Glorification of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa
beats.summary: This chapter enumerates the eighteen major Purāṇas with their verse counts, extols the Śrīmad Bhāgavatam as the supreme Purāṇa, describes its origin from Lord Viṣṇu to Brahmā, and glorifies its study and donation. It concludes with prayers to the Lord and to Śuka, the narrator.
